206.name: Male baldness
206.description: Male baldness related to genes and male sex hormones affects almost 80% of men under the age of 70 and may influence their self-esteem. Recently, however, it has also been suggested that it may be related to other androgen-related conditions.
206.overview:
Hair loss is the loss of more than 100 hairs a day. Alopecia is a hair loss so important that the thickness of the hair is visibly reduced.

The limit between natural hair loss and pathologic hair loss is not precise. A certain quantity of hair loss is totally normal since each hair is constantly being renewed. During this regeneration, the “production site” of a hair, the hair follicle and it root, goes through a three-phase cycle:

    Growth phase (anagen phase)
    Transitional phase (catagen phase)
    Shedding phase (telogen phase)

In the anagen phase, the hair is nourished from the root and grows. This cycle can last several years in healthy persons. During the growth phase, the cells are especially vulnerable to external factors. If this happens, this phase may be cut short.

During the catagen phase, which lasts only a couple of weeks, hair nourishment ceases and the cells stop dividing. The hair then enters in a rest phase (shedding), the telogen phase, lasting about four months. Towards the end of this period, the hair falls out and a new cycle begins. Alopecia can occur if the vital cycle of the hair follicle is altered.
206.symptoms: The typical pattern for male baldness begins at the hair line which gradually recedes and forms an "M". finally the hair becomes finer, thinner and shorter and makes a u-shaped pattern around the sides of the head.
206.prevention:
The prevention of male hair loss (alopecia) is conditioned by its cause. Nevertheless, generally prevention is limited or impossible.

Neither androgenic allepecia nor alopecia areata can be prevented. On the contrary, certain types of diffuse alopecia can be slowed by adopting a healthy and balanced nutrition. This impedes the development of hypoproteinemia and a deficit of iron or other nutrients that contribute to hair loss.
206.technical_description:
Androgenic alopecia is a common disorder affecting 40% of adult men and it is a highly heritable condition, with heritability estimates of over 80%. The importance of male hormones in male pattern baldness is supported by the finding that a variation in the androgen receptor gene may lower the age at which a man begins to lose hair and the degree of baldness.

The androgen receptor responds to male hormones signals such as testosterone and dihydrotestosterone. It is encoded by the AR gene, which resides on chromosome X. The influence of several polymorphisms in male pattern baldness is reflected in that baldness is often passed from parent to child.

The rs6152 polymorphism, located in the first exon of the AR gene, seems to be necessary to develop baldness, other polymorphisms also may be present to fully develop pattern baldness. In men who have the G allele in rs6152 the baldness appears faster than in men with the A allele (Ellis et al. 2007).

It has been found other polymorphisms which act independently of androgen receptor and shows no clear connection with male hormones. In 2008, Richards et al. conducted a Genome-Wide Association Study or GWAS in 1,125 men and they demonstrated an association between the locus at 20p11 and male baldness. The authors demonstrated that the rs1160312-A (LOC100270679; 20p11) allele increases the baldness risk. The allele rs2180439-T located in the 20p11 locus also increases the likelihood of suffering from baldness according to the study published by Brockschmidt et al., in 2011.

The meta-analysis published in 2012 by Li and colleagues conducted in 12,806 Europeans, confirmed some of the associations described above, such as the 20p11 locus and the AR gene, and also found other loci/genes related to the risk of baldness, of which we analyse the most significant: rs12565727 TARDBP, rs6945541 AUTS2, rs2073963 HDAC9, rs9287638 HDAC4, rs10502861 SETBP1 and rs6047844 PAX1, FOXA2.

Other gene involved in the pathogenesis, progression, and chronicity of alopecia is ULBP (cytomegalovirus UL16-binding protein) located in the chromosome 6q25.1. The protein encoded by this gene participates in the autoimmune response, polymorphism in this gene have been related to alopecia in the GWAS published by Petukhova in the Nature journal in 2010.

452.name: Cognitive ability
452.description: The study of genetic variants can help us understand the causes of differences in the cognitive functions of individuals that are determined by the environment but also have an important contribution from genetics.

458.name: Sleep duration
458.description: It is now clear that genetics plays a major role in sleep traits. Although environmental factors can influence sleep duration and intensity, certain genetic variants are known to affect these traits, and in some cases can even lead to familial sleep disorders.

565.name: Resistin levels
565.description: Resistin is a key hormone that influences insulin resistance and inflammation, critical factors in obesity and metabolic disease. Resistin levels are determined by a combination of factors, including BMI, diet, physical activity and inflammatory status. In addition, genetic factors play a crucial role in regulating resistin levels.

564.name: Cathepsin D levels
564.description: Cathepsin D is a lysosomal protease crucial for the degradation of both intracellular and extracellular proteins. This enzyme is essential for protein turnover, apoptosis and various cell signaling processes.

567.name: Lymphocyte count
567.description: Lymphocyte count is a vital indicator of a person's immune health, influenced by infections, nutritional status, stress and medical treatments. Genetics also plays a key role in lymphocyte regulation, with genetic variants that can predispose to various health conditions.

457.name: Alcohol dependence after prolonged consumption
457.description: Alcohol consumption today involves two contradictory facets. While the advantages of drinking alcohol are seen in terms of its consumption in moderation, its abuse and excessive consumption is currently a major public health problem, resulting in one of the leading causes of death and disability worldwide.

467.name: Insomnia
467.description: Insomnia refers to a chronic sleep disorder that involves difficulties in falling or staying asleep, and goes beyond sporadic sleep disturbances. This problem can have a significant impact on the quality of life and daily functioning of those who suffer from it. In fact, it is estimated that about one third of the general population has experienced insomnia at some point in their lives.

536.name: Resting heart rate
536.description: The resting heart rate is the rate at which the required amount of blood is pumped without any physical exercise. Normal values range from 50 to 100 beats per minute. It is a trait significantly influenced by genetics and correlated with life expectancy.

558.name: Red blood cell count
558.description: Red blood cell count is a vital indicator of overall health and depends on a combination of nutritional, medical, environmental and genetic factors. The genetic influence is particularly important, as it determines the body's intrinsic ability to produce and maintain an adequate level of red blood cells, directly impacting the body's ability to efficiently transport oxygen.

465.name: Epigenetic aging
465.description: Intrinsic epigenetic age acceleration refers to a phenomenon in which biological age, determined through epigenetic markers such as DNA methylation, advances at a more accelerated rate compared to a person's chronological age.

455.name: C-reactive protein levels
455.description: C-reactive protein is a protein commonly used to determine the degree of effectiveness of a treatment as it is linked to the immune system and participates in the defense system against infections.

468.name: Neuroticisms
468.description: Neuroticism is one of the main personality traits referring to a person`s tendency to experience negative emotions, such as anxiety, sadness, irritability and worry.

554.name: Gamma glutamyl transferase levels
554.description: Gamma glutamyl transferase (GGT) levels are an important clinical marker for assessing liver health and risk of metabolic disease. Factors such as genetics, alcohol consumption, certain medications, obesity, and metabolic conditions such as type 2 diabetes can influence GGT levels. Understanding these factors is essential for accurate assessment and proper management of GGT-related health conditions.

466.name: Facial aging
466.description: Skin aging is influenced by intrinsic and extrinsic factors, such as sun exposure. This leads to changes in the epidermis and dermis, causing wrinkles and other signs of aging. Extracellular matrix integrity and hydration are essential for skin health.

471.name: Risk tendency
471.description: Risk taking is a key component of several psychiatric disorders and may influence lifestyle choices such as smoking, alcohol consumption and diet.

543.name: Serum albumin levels
543.description: Albumin levels in the blood reflect the amount of this protein produced by the liver, which is essential for maintaining oncotic pressure and transporting various substances. Abnormal albumin values may indicate conditions such as liver disease, kidney disease, malnutrition or infections.

546.name: Aspartate aminotransferase levels
546.description: Aspartate aminotransferase (AST), also known as glutamic oxaloacetic transaminase (GOT), is a key enzyme in amino acid metabolism and energy production. This protein is found in various tissues of the body, mainly in the liver, heart, muscles and kidneys.

557.name: White blood cell count
557.description: The white blood cell count is a vital indicator of the health of the immune system and the general state of the body. It is influenced by a combination of intrinsic, extrinsic and genetic factors. Understanding how these factors interact to regulate white blood cell count is essential for the diagnosis, monitoring and treatment of various diseases, as well as for the development of personalized therapeutic interventions.

562.name: Usual walking pace
562.description: Habitual walking pace is a key indicator of health and longevity, influenced by environmental, lifestyle and genetic factors. Walking at a faster pace is associated with better physical fitness and a lower risk of cardiometabolic disease. Recent studies have identified genetic variants that predispose to a faster walking pace, suggesting that walking faster could be a beneficial intervention to improve overall health. Promoting not only more walking, but also faster walking, is essential for better public health.

456.name: Eye clarity
456.description: Eye color is determined by the abundance, density and distribution of melanin in the iris, being higher in dark eyes than in light ones. In addition, there are other extracellular factors that determine the absorption and scattering of light, which also contribute to give the iris its color. This is a genetically very complex human trait in which numerous genes are involved.

227.name: QT Intervals
227.description: The cardiac QT interval (QTc) determines the time elapsed between contraction and relaxation of the cardiac ventricles. It may be shortened at faster heart rates, or lengthened at slower rates without further consequences, however, abnormalities in this interval may trigger clinical implications.
227.overview:
Long QT syndrome

Long QT syndrome (LQTS) is a genetic disease associated with abnormal cardiac ion channel function, which affects individuals without evident cardiac structure anomalies and which manifests itself through the lengthening of the QT interval on the electrocardiogram

Congenital long QT interval is a family affectation, affecting 1/5,000 persons, and is the principal cause of sudden death in children and adolescents.

The space between the start of the Q wave and the end of the T wave (Q-T interval) corresponds to the time it takes for your heart to contract and then refill with blood before beginning the next contraction. Long QT syndrome is characterized by a prolonged repolarization. In it, your heart muscle takes longer than normal to recharge between beats. This electrical disturbance, which often can be seen on an electrocardiogram (ECG), is called a prolonged Q-T interval. Alterations can lead to syncope, ventricular tachycardia, torsade de pointes, cardiac arrest and sudden death.
227.symptoms:
Those persons affected by long QT syndrome do not always show symptoms. Where these do occur, the most common one are fainting and arrhythmia. Persons with long QT often present a prolonged QT interval during physical exercise, in moments of emotional stress or as a reaction to a loud or alarming sound.

The persons who suffer from LQTS have generally had at least one fainting episode before their 10th birthday. Other may have only one or two fainting episodes during childhood but no others afterwards.

Since LQT may produce an abnormal cardiac rhythm (arrhythmia), fainting or loss of consciousness (syncope) and even sudden death, it is important to seek medical attention for a child who has the above symptoms during physical exercise, intense emotional moments, swimming or brusque auditory stimulations.

Generally, Patients under clinical suspicion of long QT syndrome: asymptomatic subjects with serial ECGs showing QTc
VALUES >460 ms (prepubescent) or > 470 ms in adult women and 450 ms in adult males. The QT interval is best measured in the II and V5 leads of the ECG.
227.prevention:
A medical test is recommended for those subjects with

    an ECG characteristic of the syndrome
    a personal or family history of sudden death or suspicion of LQT
    subjects with a history of syncope of unknown origin
    subjects with ventricular fibrillation of unknown origin

There are no specific measures to prevent Long QT syndrome given the genetic importance but a specific study and follow-up is recommended for those persons who from young ages have had any syncope, especially when brought on by exercise, strong emotions or loud auditory stimulations.
227.technical_description:
This rs10494366 polymorphism is a SNP localized in the gene encoding the protein NOS1AP (nitric oxide synthase adapter protein 1), responsible for some of the variations studied in abnormal heart rhythms, in particular, the QT interval.

It is known that about 35% of the variation in the QT interval is heritable. In a recent genome-wide association study, a common polymorphism (rs10494366) located in the NOS1AP gene, determined its association with the variation of the QT interval. The rs10494366 (G) allele (36% frequency) was associated with 3.8 ms (confidence interval 95%, 3.0 to 4.6; P=7.8x10(-20)) with increased QTc interval duration for each additional copy allele. However, despite this association determine the duration of the QT interval, no significant association with increased sudden cardiac death was found. However, larger sample sizes are needed to confirm or discart this effect on sudden cardiac death.

469.name: Probability of snoring
469.description: Snoring is a loud, vibrating noise that occurs during sleep due to a partial blockage in the upper airway. In some cases, snoring may be a sign of a more serious breathing disorder, such as sleep apnea.

540.name: Telomere length
540.description: Telomeres are DNA-protein structures found at the ends of chromosomes whose function is to maintain genomic stability and cellular life. Their length is a hereditary biomarker that can provide information about genetic aging.

538.name: Corneal hysteresis
538.description: Corneal hysteresis is a measure that assesses the ability of the cornea to absorb and release energy, reflecting its elasticity and viscosity. It is determined by observing how the cornea deforms under pressure and how it returns to its original shape. This parameter is crucial in the evaluation of ocular health, particularly in relation to glaucoma and its progression.

550.name: Alkaline phosphatase levels
550.description: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is an enzyme found in various tissues of the body, with particularly high concentrations in the liver, bile ducts, bones, kidneys and intestine. This enzyme plays an important role in numerous physiological processes, including bone and liver metabolism.

563.name: Spleen volume
563.description: The spleen is a key lymphatic organ located in the left upper abdomen, playing essential roles in immunity and blood filtration. Spleen volume, which can vary significantly between individuals and under different physiological and pathological conditions, is a crucial indicator of your overall health and functional status.

220.name: Height
220.description: Height is the height of an individual from head to toe. It is a complex trait influenced by genetic and environmental factors. It is a characteristic that shows great heterogeneity and can vary according to age, sex, geographic region or ethnicity.
220.overview:
Height is defined as a person's height, measured from head to foot.

The height of a person is a complex character conditioned by multiple factors.  It is estimated that more than 80% of the variation in a population can be attributed to genetic factors. Other factors are also involved, such as sex, population, parental height, and environmental factors, among which nutrition is of great importance.

Height, like many other biological variables, acts as a continuous variable, and its values show a "normal" distribution that can be represented as a Gaussian bell. Statistically, normal heights are those between ± 2 standard deviations (SD) for age, sex, and ethnicity. Tall sizes are those between + 2 SD and short sizes are considered to be those below 2 SD.

Two groups are distinguished: normal short stature, which includes children with familial short stature, with constitutional delay in growth and development or with delayed puberty; and short stature secondary to a pathological cause. A height below -2 SD does not necessarily imply that it is pathological.

Factors that may affect height:

Family history: parental heights, age of menarche in the mother, age of onset of shaving or voice change in the father, the existence of short or tall stature in grandparents and uncles, as well as delayed or early puberty.

Personal background: pregnancy, gestational age, whether the birth was normal or presented perinatal asphyxia, neonatal weight, and height to rule out intrauterine growth retardation.
220.technical_description:
The height is influenced by genes and environment (mainly nutrition), but some studies suggest that 80% of the height variation is determined by genes. Several polymorphisms are being studied in relation to height.

One study measured the height of more than 23,000 people of European descent and genotype them to find SNPs associated with height. The rs1042725 (C) allele (located on HMGA2 gene) has been associated with 0.4 cm height (0.16 inches).

The SNP rs6060369 is associated with a slight increase in height (0,44cm) in individuals carrying the allele rs6060369 (C), being statistically significant (P=9.7x10(-7)) on a sample of more than 28,000 individuals.

Another study performed in approximately 1,200 Swedish men aged (age 70) found that individuals homozygous for the rs2179922 (G;G) polymorphism were between 0.90 to 2.3 cm higher than the other variants. There was no statistical association for the women studied.

Rs9834312 polymorphism is a SNP localized in the FLNB gene. Based on a study of more than 1,000 Caucasians and over 2,000 Chinese, genotype (G;G) of this polymorphism was associated with an increase in height in both Caucasian populations (P =0.008) and the Chinese population (P=0.017).

539.name: Intensity of itching due to mosquito bites
539.description: Mosquito bites are itchy bumps that form on the skin after the blood is sucked out by these invertebrates. The reaction the body has to this bite depends on the sensitivity a person shows, which depends, among other factors, on individual genetics.

215.name: Nicotine dependence after prolonged consumption
215.description: Smoking remains the leading cause of preventable death worldwide. It consists of a multistage process, for which nicotine dependence is the limiting phase of smoking cessation, characterized by a compulsive desire to smoke and loss of control over consumption despite the consequences.
215.overview:
Nicotine addiction is an important phenomena in today´s world:

    Almost 15 million Spanish adults smoke (34.4% of the population over 16 years of age say they smoke).
    Between 60 and 70% of them are addicted to nicotine.
    Tobacco is responsible for approximately 56,000 deaths annually in Spain; for 36% deaths from cancer, for 20% from cardiovascular illnesses and for 80% from chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD).

There are nicotine receptors in all of the nervous system, central as well as the peripheral, which is why the effect of this drug takes place not only in the brain but also in the muscular fibers of the intestine and the colon.

On inhaling tobacco smoke the associated tar particles carry the nicotine to the lungs from where it passes to the bloodstream and later to the brain.

The addiction mechanism is activated when the nicotine is delivered to the lungs by inhaling tobacco smoke. It then reaches the brain neurons which liberate a substance known as GABA (gamma-aminobutyric acid) which, in turn, stimulate the neurons to release another substance, dopamine. If these neurons are overexcited by the nicotine, the secretion of dopamine is limited. On the long term the neurons adapt their metabolism and need a greater quantity of nicotine to produce the same initial effect.

Why you should quit smoking:

    Breathing improves and fatigue lessens
    Coughing spells and predisposition to infections diminish
    The skin and face recover from premature aging
    Sense of smell and taste improve
    Deterioration of lung function slows down
    Risk of heart disease and sudden death are reduced in a 20-50%
    Three years after quitting, the risk of a heart attack or a stroke of an ex-smoker is practically the same as that of a non-smoker.
    Important economic reasons: currently to smoke almost a pack a day means an average expense of more than 1500€ a year.
215.symptoms:
Nicotine addiction affects the body both physically and psychologically; some effects are due to its use and others are caused by nicotine withdrawal.

Nicotine use

Nicotine produces certain symptoms in persons with nicotine addiction (nicotine dependence) as well as in persons who smoke only sporadically. Nicotine stimulates, slows down; stresses and relaxes the body all at the same time.

    Under the effects of nicotine the heat beats more rapidly, blood pressure increases, blood flow to the skin and internal organs is reduced and the body temperature lowers.
    When the body metabolizes nicotine, it produces nicotinic acid (niacin), one of the B-complex vitamins which dilates blood vessels and increases psychomotor performance.

Nicotine withdrawal

The lack of nicotine leads to a series of symptoms called withdrawal syndrome. These symptoms can include the following: nervousness, irritability, depressed or bad mood, aggressiveness, fear, difficulty concentrating,anxiety, insomnia, nausea, hunger and a need to smoke.

These symptoms are mainly physical resulting from nicotine addiction and tend to disappear in three to four weeks.
215.prevention:
The best way to quit smoking is to never start and quitting is the most important thing a smoker can do to improve his/her health.

The most effective methods to avoid tobacco use include prohibiting the sale of tobacco to minors, a complete prohibition of publicity and sponsorship by tobacco companies, and the establishment of spaces 100% tobacco-free to denaturalize tobacco use, reinforced by public health campaigns in the media to advise of the negative consequences of smoking; the use of packaging with graphic warnings; and the creation of high impact publicity campaigns in the media to warn of the dangers of smoking.

If you smoke and wish to quit, your doctor can help you by providing adequate information and different strategies to reach your goal. If, additionally, you have an illness which can be aggravated by tobacco (coronary heart disease, high blood pressure, COPD, diabetes, etc.) it is imperative to quit smoking as a principal preventive measure.

The majority of people who have quit smoking have done it on their own, alone and without more help than their own motivation and with the support of their family and friends. Many people who try to quit smoking only do so after the second or third attempt. For some people, a nicotine substitute treatment is a valuable aid and there are also diverse commercial support therapies that have helped others quit smoking.
215.technical_description:
The rs3003609 is a SNP or genetic polymorphism located in the DNM1 gene. The rs3003609-T allele is associated with increased smoking and thus nicotine dependence, at least in Caucasians (based on a study of 600 families), although in African Americans it has not been demonstrated.

A study of cases (688 Caucasian smokers) and controls (non-smokers) indicated that the rs10485057 allele (G) was associated with an increased likelihood of smoking initiation and nicotine dependence. It is a SNP located in the opioid receptor, MU 1 of the OPRM1 gene.

Males homozygous for the G allele of the rs1044396 polymorphism (GG genotype) are at increased risk for developing nicotine addiction. Carrying the rs1044396-A allele is associated with a normal/standard risk for the development of nicotine addiction.

Individuals carrying the rs1801272-T allele have a higher risk of starting to smoke, and being smokers, these individuals have a higher risk of smoking than average. On the other hand, individuals homozygous for rs1801272 (AA genotype) have a normal risk of smoking initiation.

The rs1051730 polymorphism, also known as D398N, is a SNP located in the nicotinic acetylcholine receptor alpha 3 subunit of the CHRNA3 gene. Several studies have concluded that carriers of the T allele of the rs1051730 SNP are not at increased risk of becoming smokers compared to (C) carriers. However, (T) carriers are significantly more likely to smoke more cigarettes than (C) carrier individuals, and as an obvious consequence, are at increased risk of lung cancer as reported in this and other studies. Therefore, this study links rs1051730 directly to increased nicotine dependence, and indirectly to increased lung cancer risk.

The rs1051730 polymorphism, also known as D398N, is a SNP located in the nicotinic acetylcholine receptor alpha 3 subunit of the CHRNA3 gene. Several studies have concluded that carriers of the T allele of the rs1051730 SNP are not at increased risk of becoming smokers compared to (C) carriers. However, (T) carriers are significantly more likely to smoke more cigarettes than (C) carrier individuals, and as an obvious consequence, are at increased risk of lung cancer as reported in this and other studies. This study therefore links rs1051730 directly to increased nicotine dependence, and indirectly to increased lung cancer risk.

A recent study tentatively identified numerous genetic variants as risk factors for the transition from smoking to the development of nicotine dependence, including an amino acid change in the cholinergic nicotinic cholinergic receptor alpha5 (CHRNA5). The purpose of this study was to replicate these findings in an independent data set and further investigate the role of genetic variation in the physically linked nicotinic receptor cluster, CHRNA5-CHRNA3-CHRNB4, and smoking risk. Individuals from 219 European American families (N = 2,284) were genotyped across this gene cluster to test for genetic association with smoking. The frequency of the rs16969968 or c.1192 G>A variant was studied in 995 individuals from diverse ethnic populations. In vitro studies were carried out to directly test whether this variant of the CHRNA5 gene influences nicotinic receptor function. The result was that individuals carrying the rs16969968-A allele had an increased propensity to smoke among smokers.

470.name: Morning circadian rythm (Morning person)
470.description: Being a morning person is related to waking up early and having more energy during the morning and is a trait that has sometimes been linked to mood, productivity and overall health.

464.name: Dental caries and periodontitis
464.description: Tooth decay and periodontitis are two common but distinct oral health problems that affect the teeth and gums. Both can have serious consequences if not treated properly.

542.name: Alanine aminotransferase levels
542.description: Alanine aminotransferase (ALT) is a liver enzyme involved in energy production. When liver cells are damaged, ALT is released into the bloodstream, resulting in increased levels in the blood. Therefore, ALT levels act as a sensitive indicator of liver damage.

472.name: Mouth ulcers
472.description: Mouth ulcers are a common disorder whose incidence decreases with age and can have multiple causes, some of which are still unknown. They are often painful and can make eating and speaking difficult.

559.name: Monocyte count
559.description: Monocyte counts are crucial to the health of the immune system, as these white blood cells fight infection and maintain immune balance. Genetic and environmental factors such as infections, inflammatory diseases and stress can influence their levels.

551.name: Estradiol levels
551.description: Levels of estradiol, the most potent form of estrogen, vary significantly between men and women and throughout the stages of life. Alterations in estradiol levels may be related to risk of thrombosis, certain types of cancer and osteoporosis.

453.name: Mental agility
453.description: In logical reasoning, the ideas or sequence of facts are developed coherently and without contradiction. Logical ability is the ability that allows us to think and reason quickly when faced with a problem, without prior information and experience. Its measurement can be very useful in assessing a person's psychological abilities.

560.name: Neutrophil count
560.description: The neutrophil count is a crucial indicator of immune health and the body's ability to fight infection. This count depends on a variety of factors, including age, gender, health status, and medication.

555.name: Selectin E levels
555.description: E selectin is essential for the inflammatory process and the immune response, facilitating the adhesion and migration of leukocytes to sites of inflammation. Its levels are influenced by a combination of inflammatory factors, health conditions and lifestyle habits, as well as genetic determinants.

463.name: Acne vulgaris
463.description: Acne vulgaris is a common skin disorder caused by inflammation of the pilosebaceous unit, resulting in the characteristic comedones, papules, pustules, nodules and cysts.

425.name: Thyroid function (TSH levels)
425.description: Thyroid dysfunction is an important health problem that affects 10% of the population, increasing, above all, cardiovascular risk. Among other parameters, in clinical practice it is evaluated by measuring TSH levels, so that an increase would be related to hypothyroidism and a decrease to hyperthyroidism.
425.overview:
The thyroid is a bilobed gland butterfly shaped placed in the neck, in front of the proximal trachea. The left and right lobes extend along either side of the trachea and larynx close to the carotid arteries.

The hypothalamus stimulates the pituitary gland through thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH) to secrete the pituitary hormone thyrotropin (TSH) that is released to the blood and stimulates the thyroid follicular cells. These cells release thyroxine or T4, which is the inactive hormone that is converted to active thyroid hormone T3. Clinically thyroid function is evaluated though free thyroxine T4 and TSH levels.

Thyroid hormone increases cell metabolism and is involved in basal metabolic rate, cardiac and respiratory rate, fetal development, nervous system stimulation, reproductive health, and other endocrine organ functions.

Twin and family studies suggest that thyroid function is highly heritable, up to 65%. To date, several GWAS and meta-analysis found more than 15 loci related to thyroid function.
425.technical_description:
The TSH hormone stimulates thyroid follicular cells through the interaction with the TSH receptor or TSHR that activates cyclic AMP (cAMP) signaling and the expression of several target genes involved in T4 production. The thyroid produces approximately 90% inactive thyroid prohormone T4 and 10% active thyroid hormone, or triiodothyronine (T3). Inactive thyroid hormone is converted peripherally to either activated thyroid hormone in the liver and kidney, through the process of deiodination. T4 and T3 hormone levels exert negative feedback on TSH levels. High T4/T3 levels directly inhibit TSH synthesis and release from the anterior pituitary, thus decreasing TSH bloodstream levels (Mariotti, 2016).

Discrete and moderate variation in TSH levels seems to influence health outcomes. There is growing evidence that higher levels of TSH are associated with worsening blood pressure increasing cardiovascular risk. Variation in thyroid function has a substantial impact on weight and body mass index or BMI and the rise in TSH levels has been related to increased weight. Furthermore, variation in TSH levels seems to affect glomerular filtration rate, in the kidney, and the risk of osteoporosis and bone fracture. Pregnancy outcome and cancer risk seem also to have a relationship with the fluctuation in TSH levels (Taylor et al., 2013).

In the last decade, genome-wide association studies (GWAS) and meta-analysis focused on identifying common variants associated with TSH levels, in other words, variants that are frequent in the population and may influence the TSH levels.

All common variants identified explained around 20% of the variance in TSH levels (Taylor et al., 2014). We analyze 4 of the most relevant variants.

The PDE8B (phosphodiesterase 8B) gene encodes for a high-affinity cAMP-specific phosphodiesterase that catalyzes the hydrolysis of the second messenger cAMP and is highly expressed in the thyroid tissue. The cAMP-specific PDE4 family (that includes the PDE8B enzyme) has been suggested to be involved in modulating the cAMP signal after the TSH stimulation of thyrocytes. In 2008 Arnaud-Lopez et al. published a study that included in 6,148 Sardinians showing that SNPs located in the PDE8B gene seemed to be related to the TSH levels and consequently with thyroid function. They suggested that PDE8B polymorphisms probably affect the cAMP concentrations in the thyroid, and therefore serum thyroid-hormone levels affecting TSH release from the pituitary. The rs4704397 SNP in PDE8B reached the strongest genome-wide significant association. Each copy of the rs4704397-A allele was found to be associated with an increase in the TSH concentration of 0.13 mIU/l. They also showed that it seems that the rs4704397-A allele has a larger effect on thyroid-affected individuals in comparison to persons with normal TSH levels that did not report having thyroid disease, neither taking thyroid medications. However, this finding did not reach statistical significance and larger studies should be performed in thyroid-affected persons. It has been found that the rs4704397 polymorphism may explain 1.82% of the variance observed in TSH levels (Rawal., 2012).

After Arnau-Lopez et al. study, two meta-analyses carried by Porcu et al. (2013) and Rawal et al. (2012) identified other common variants associated with serum TSH levels such as rs10917477 (CAPZB), rs10028213 (NR3C2), and rs3813582 (16q23) and corroborated the previous association of PDE8B gene with thyroid function in European Americans and African Americans samples (Malinowski et al., 2014; Taylor et al., 2015). It is important to note that rs4704397 polymorphism, described previously, is in linkage disequilibrium with rs2046045 and rs2928167 (reported in Porcu and Rawal meta-analyses).

The rs10917477 polymorphism located in position 1:19534612 close to the CAPZB (capping actin protein of muscle Z-line subunit beta) was related to thyroid function. This locus encodes the beta subunit of the barbed-end F-actin binding protein. The rs10917477-G allele was associated with decreased TSH levels and explain a 0.42% variance in TSH levels (Rawal., 2012).

According to Rawal et al. findings, the 0.59% variance in TSH levels may be explained by rs10028213 polymorphism, located in 4:148731458 position, close to NR3C2 gene (nuclear receptor subfamily 3 group C member 2 gene) which encodes for a mineralocorticoid receptor that is involved generally in the response to aldosterone hormone. Once NR3C2 interacts with aldosterone, it is translocated to the cell nucleus where acts as a transcription factor regulating genes involved in salt and water balance. The authors indicate that probably rs10028213 regulates the NR3C2 expression. However, the molecular mechanism underlying NR3C2 expression and its possible effect on TSH levels remain unclear.

The rs3813582 SNP is in a desert region (16q23) that seems to be related to TSH levels. The rs3813582-T allele may increase the TSH concentration and explains 0.48% of the variance in TSH levels. The rs3813582 SNP is located downstream of the functional gene (LOC440389) associated with thyroid volume (Rawal et al., 2013).

247.name: Birth weight
247.description: The normal weight of a newborn oscillates according to gestational age and there are reference values and percentiles that determine the appropriate weight. Both excessive and insufficient birth weight in the baby can, in some cases, cause complications that require closer pediatric monitoring for the newborn or the mother.
247.overview:
Healthy newborn babies, even if they do not have any apparent problems, have many needs and more or less routine procedures. A careful evaluation of their general state of health and implementation of correct nutrition is important.

Newborns are considered apparently healthy if they are full-term (equal to or more than 37 weeks of gestation), and their background (family, gestational and pre-natal), physical examination and adaptation certify it.

A normal weight at birth is between 2500 gr. and 4000 gr. An underweight newborn is a full-term baby but has a birth weight of less than 2500 gr.

The identification of babies who are small for gestational age (SGA) is important because they have a greater risk of perinatal morbidity, and cardiovascular disorders as an adult. A baby who is small for gestational age describes SGA (small for gestational age) generally describes any infant whose birth weight and/or length was less than the 3rd percentile. An underweight newborn has a birth weight of less than 2500 gr.

A baby diagnosed with fetal macrosomia has a birth weight of more than 4000 gr., above the average weight of a full-term baby.

Fetal macrosomia is associated with a greater incidence of caesarian births and the mortality rate is higher. The metabolic complications in babies with fetal macrosomia (constitutionally bigger) are not more frequent than in normal weight newborns, but it is not always certain that a baby with fetal macrosomia will be healthy as it sometimes indicates that the gestational control was not correct.

Although it occurs even normal populations, diverse studies link fetal macrosomia to children of diabetic or obese mothers. These children may have a greater risk of developing type 2 diabetes mellitus, obesity and a metabolic disorder in infancy or adulthood.
247.technical_description:
It is estimated that the genetics of each individual can represent about 40% of the variation in birth weight.
Rs1051730 polymorphism has been associated with the birth weight of the baby in female smokers during pregnancy. Pregnant smoker women carrying one copy of the A allele for this polymorphism are more likely to have babies that weigh, on average, less at birth. Women who present two copies of the A allele (AA genotype) have a higher probability that the baby weighs less.
Another polymorphism associated with the baby's weight (regardless of mother smoking status), is the rs1799884. In a study covering the birth weight of almost 7,000 children and the genotype of their mothers, it was concluded that each T allele represents an increase in birth weight of approximately 27 grams in neonates. Thus, mothers with a CT genotype, on average, had babies weighing about 27 grams more, and mothers with a TT genotype had babies that on average weighed 54 g more.
Another polymorphism associated with the weight of babies is the rs7903146. In a study in which 8,344 mother-baby couples were studied, it was determined that, on average, mothers carrying at least one copy of the C allele for this polymorphism had babies weighing about 27 grams more. Those mothers who presented two copies of the C allele (genotype CC) had babies that, on average, weighed approximately 54 grams more

537.name: Frequency of bowel movements
537.description: Stool frequency is related to gastrointestinal function, being a significant marker of digestive tract motility. The number of bowel movements can vary considerably and is influenced by environmental factors such as diet and lifestyle.

534.name: Liver iron levels
534.description: The liver is one of the main storage sites for iron, an essential element for life, although its overload is toxic. Excessive iron content in the liver has been linked to various liver and metabolic diseases such as hereditary hemochromatosis.

561.name: Eosinophil count
561.description: Eosinophil count is an important measure in the evaluation of immune status and in the detection of various diseases. It is influenced by allergic, infectious, autoimmune and environmental factors. Genetic predisposition also plays a crucial role in the regulation of eosinophil count, highlighting the importance of genetics in the body's immune response.

454.name: Sex hormone regulation (SHBG)
454.description: Sex hormone-binding globulin is a steroid-binding protein that is affected by various factors such as age and can serve as an indicator of endocrine disorders or metabolic diseases.

460.name: Basal metabolic rate
460.description: Even when you are at complete rest, your body is still working to maintain those vital functions that are necessary. The basal metabolic rate is the minimum amount of energy your body needs to survive by performing these basic functions, such as breathing, blinking, filtering blood, regulating temperature or synthesizing hormones.

435.name: Skin melanin levels
435.description: Skin color is a highly variable trait with a wide spectrum of possibilities from almost black to almost white. It is determined by the amount and type of melanin and is closely related to eye and hair color. This color variation is mostly due to genetics.
435.technical_description:
The colour of the skin is determined by the melanosomes, intracellular organelles of the melanocytes that contain melanin. This brown-black pigment is responsible for absorbing UV radiation and transforming it into callous. The colouring of the skin is determined by the number, size, and shape of the melanosomes and the amount and type of melatonin. Although some genes that contribute physiologically to skin colour have been described, the SLC24A5 gene has been cataloged as the most important.

The SLC24A5 gene (located on the long arm of chromosome 15) encodes for the NCKX5 protein, a membrane transporter of the Na-K exchanger family located in the Golgi apparatus of melanocytes and involved in melanin biosynthesis. Disruption studies show how the elimination of this protein causes a significant reduction in melanin biogenesis.

The rs1426654 variant of the SLC24A5 gene, also known as c.331A>G or p.Thr111Ala, is directly related to the determination of skin colour. In particular, the A allele is associated with a lighter skin phenotype and is characteristic of Europeans and Western Asians. In contrast, the G allele is associated with a darker skin tone and is more present in Asian or African individuals.

The other variant of the same gene analysed, the rs16891982, also known as c.1122G>C or p.Leu374Phe, is also associated with skin colour. In this, the G allele (oriented according to dbSNP) is associated with light skin colour and is typical of European descent, whereas the C allele is associated with a dark colour and is especially frequent in the African and South Asian population.
435.technical_citations:
Valenzuela R. et al. Predicting phenotype from genotype: normal pigmentation. J Forensic Sci. 2010 Mar 1;55(2):315-22.
Beleza S. et al. Genetic architecture of skin and eye color in an African-European admixed population. PLoS Genet. 2013 Mar;9(3):e1003372.
Lamason R. et al. SLC24A5, a putative cation exchanger, affects pigmentation in zebrafish and humans. Science. 2005 Dec 16;310(5755):1782-6.

461.name: Hair color
461.description: Hair pigmentation, similar to skin pigmentation, is an aspect determined mainly by the amount and distribution of melanin. The proportion and amount of melanin is specific to each individual, resulting in a wide variety of hair colorations.

535.name: Corneal curvature
535.description: The cornea is fundamental for vision, since its curvature directly influences light refraction and, therefore, visual quality. Alterations in this curvature can result in refractive errors such as myopia and hyperopia. Refractive errors are widespread in the world and one of the most affected populations is the Asian population.

473.name: Left-handedness (left lateral)
473.description: Left-handedness is the preference for using primarily the left hand to perform everyday activities, such as writing, eating, or throwing an object. Left handedness is less common than right handedness and has been linked to certain neuropsychiatric traits such as schizophrenia and bipolar disorder.

541.name: Total serum protein levels
541.description: Serum total protein level measures the total amount of protein in the blood, mainly divided into albumin and globulins. A proper balance is crucial, as both high and low levels can indicate various clinical conditions, including liver problems, kidney problems, or malnutrition. Generally, normal serum total protein levels are usually between 6.0 and 8.3 g/dL, although this range may vary depending on the laboratory and individual factors such as age and sex.

221.name: Hair Shape
221.description: The pattern or shape of the hair is influenced by the circumference of the hair, which in turn is determined by the structure of the fiber and its protein composition. Thus, this parameter determines its own characteristics, influences the care or treatment it may need, and affects the ability to maintain its length.
221.overview:
The average diameter of a human adult´s hair is around 70 micras (0.70 mm), being very thick when it is 0.1 mm and very fine when it is 0.025 mm. One important fact is that as a person ages, the diameter of the hair grows smaller; it loses thickness.

When we talk about thickness we are referring to the coarseness of each individual strand of hair, not to the totality of hair on a person´s head.

Thickness, as well as the type of hair, are important characteristics to be aware of because both affect the ability to grow long hair. For example, coarse hair has the largest circumference, making it the strongest of all hair textures. This makes it easier to grow long because it is more resistant to damage.

To discover your hair type, you have to take a strand of hair (from a comb or brush) and hold it to the light:

    Coarse hair: Thick strands whose shed strands usually are easily identified against most backgrounds;
    Medium: Strands that are neither coarse nor fine; medium hair feels like a cotton thread. You can feel it, but it isn´t stiff or rough.
    Fine hair: Thin strands that sometimes are almost translucent when held up to the light.
221.technical_description:
The rs11803731 (c.2368T>A) is a polymorphism or SNP located in the TCHH gene, which codes for trichohyalin, a protein found in the roots of the hair follicle. In a study of Australian individuals of European descent, carriers of the rs11803731-A allele were associated with an increased likelihood of curly hair, while individuals homozygous for the T allele (TT genotype) tended to have straight hair.

The rs17646946 is a polymorphism located on chromosome 1 that is apparently part of the trichohyalin (TCHHL) gene. Trichohyalin is a protein found in the roots of the hair follicle. In a study in which the association of this polymorphism with hair shape was studied, it was determined that individuals carrying the rs17646946-A allele were more likely to have straight hair, as opposed to individuals with the GG genotype who were more likely to have curly hair.

A study of 5,386 individuals of European ancestry identified a genetic variant associated with curly hair, the rs7349332 or c.757-1113C>T polymorphism. In this study, each copy of the rs7349332-T allele was associated with the presence of slightly curly hair.

547.name: Bilirubin levels
547.description: Bilirubin is the end product of the breakdown of hemoglobin from aged red blood cells. Bilirubin levels in the blood are an important indicator of liver function.

512.name: Heat production in response to cold
512.description: Thermogenesis is a vital process for the maintenance of body temperature and energy balance in mammals. Research on BAT (brown adipose tissue) and its activation pathways offers promising prospects for the treatment and prevention of obesity and its related metabolic complications.

240.name: PSA (Prostate Specific Antigen) Levels
240.description: The serum PSA value is useful for the follow-up and early detection of prostate cancer since the probability of developing prostate cancer increases with higher values. However, since there is no agreed cut-off point, additional tests are often required to establish the diagnosis or to rule out other causes of its increase.
240.overview:
Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) is a protein synthesized exclusively by the prostate gland. A small amount is found in the blood stream and serves for the diagnosis, prognosis and follow-up of prostate cancer and other prostate disorders such as prostatitis. PSA analysis has been widely used to detect prostate cancer in men. It is also used to monitor men who have been diagnosed with prostate cancer to check for reoccurrence after the initial treatment or if there is a reaction to the treatment.

The higher the concentration of PSA, the greater is the possibility that a man has prostate cancer or some other problem related with the prostate gland. Nonetheless there are other reasons for an elevated concentration of PSA without there being a malignant process. There can also be men with prostate cancer who do not have high concentrations of PSA.

The reference
VALUES for PSA serum vary depending on the different laboratories although the normal accepted value at present is up to 2.5 ng/ml. Its production depends on the presence of androgens and the size of the prostate gland.
240.technical_description:
Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) is a protein produced by prostate cells. Prostate cancer is not the only condition that can increase PSA levels; it could be also caused by benign prostatic hyperplasia, inflammation of the prostate gland, handling during rectal examination or sexual activity. In addition, genetic factors may influence baseline PSA levels in men, which may have implications for determining from that individual value can be acquired certain preventive measures.

It has been studied several polymorphism related to PSA levels, some of them are summarised below.

Baseline PSA levels were associated with the presence of several polymorphisms (rs401681, rs1099394 and rs11067228) in a study conducted in 9,000 men with European descent who did not have prostate cancer.

The subjects with rs401681 (C;C) genotype had higher levels of PSA (7%) compared to men who had the genotype (C;T) and (T;T). In subjects with rs1099394 (T;T) genotype PSA levels were highest (9%) in comparison to the genotypes (C;T) and (C;C). The rs11067228 (A;A) subjects had higher levels of PSA (8%) on average compared to men who had the genotype (A;G) and (G;G).

The researchers suggested that the cut points for individual PSA levels for prostate biopsy could be based on the presence of such genetic variants.

424.name: Permanent tooth eruption
424.description: Dental maturation is the process that includes the loss of primary teeth and the eruption and calcification of permanent teeth. This process of tooth replacement begins in infancy and ends practically at the onset of puberty and can be influenced by numerous variables that determine both the time elapsed and the tooth structure.
424.overview:
Permanent tooth eruption is a process in which the permanent teeth replace the primary dentition. Dental lamina is a structure originated in the oral epithelium during the embryonic period and indicates the localization of the future dentition. Permanent tooth germs are observed during development between 10th and 13th weeks of gestation but it is not until childhood, between 6 and 13 years of age when permanent tooth replace the primary dentition.

The permanent teeth develop from an extension of the dental lamina, named successional lamina, and substitute the primary tooth. The posterior permanent molars are the only teeth that do not replace any primary teeth.

Dental maturity is the number of permanent teeth at a given age that can be influenced by several factors such as genetics, gender (permanent tooth eruption occurs earlier in girls than in boys), hormonal factors, nutrition, caries or trauma to the primary teeth and ethnicity.
424.technical_description:
Permanent tooth eruption is a process in which permanent teeth replace the primary dentition. The permanent teeth develop from an extension of the dental lamina, called the successional lamina. The posterior permanent molars are the only teeth that do not replace any primary teeth (Juuri and Balic, 2017).

In 2011, Geller et al. published the first GWAS related to the time it takes for permanent tooth eruption to occur. Using dental data from women aged 6-14 years, they identified four single nucleotide polymorphisms or SNPs strongly associated with eruption time as follows: 1) The polymorphism rs2281845, located near the gene CACNA1S ("voltage-dependent calcium channel, L type") on chromosome 1q32. 1. 2) Rs4491709, which is an intron variant on chromosome 2q35. 3) Rs7924176, on 10q22.2, is located in an intronic region of the ADK (adenosine kinase) gene and could be linked to the expression of the ADK gene. 4) The variant rs12424086, at 12q14.3, is located near the HMGA2 gene ("high-mobility group AT-hook 2") which encodes a protein that acts as a transcription regulating factor.

Little is known about the function in tooth development of the four genes/regions indicated previously. Pillas et al. found that rs12424086 was associated with the regulation of primary dentition, this supports the theory that the processes that regulate primary dentition and permanent dentition development have some genes in common.

In the Geller et al. study they analysed the combined effects for all four identified SNPs between 6 and 14 years of age. They showed that the mean of women with at least four “delayed tooth eruption alleles” are prone to have less number of permanent teeth and that the permanent number of teeth decrease with the number of delayed tooth eruption alleles for the age period studied. The highest variation was found in the women at age 10–12 years group, women with 3-5 copies of the delayed tooth eruption alleles had 30 or 31 permanent teeth instead of 32, if they were carriers of 6-8 alleles, they showed an average of 15 permanent teeth and if they carried more than 9 alleles they had less than 7 permanent teeth.
424.technical_citations:
Geller F, Feenstra B, Zhang H, Shaffer JR, Hansen T, Esserlind A-L, et al. Genome-Wide Association Study Identifies Four Loci Associated with Eruption of Permanent Teeth. Gibson G, editor. PLoS Genet. 2011;7(9):e1002275.
Juuri E, Balic A. The Biology Underlying Abnormalities of Tooth Number in Humans. J. Dent. Res. 2017;96(11):1248–56.

Pillas D, Hoggart CJ, Evans DM, O’Reilly PF, Sipilä K, Lähdesmäki R, et al. Genome-Wide Association Study Reveals Multiple Loci Associated with Primary Tooth Development during Infancy. Gibson G, editor. PLoS Genet. 2010;6(2):e1000856.

430.name: Earlobe type
430.description: The separation of the earlobe from the skin of the head is a polygenic trait that is largely influenced by the genetic factor and the origin of the individual.
430.overview:
Some traits are controlled by genes that pass from parent to child. Others are acquired through learning. But most are influenced by a combination of genes and environmental factors.

If earlobes hang free, they are detached. If they connect directly to the sides of the head, they are attached. Earlobe attachment is a continuous trait: while most earlobes can be neatly categorized as attached or unattached, some are in-between.

A clear example of the attached earlobe is the actor Clint Eastwood.

Although some sources say that this trait is controlled by a single gene, there are no published studies to support this view. Attachment and the shape of the earlobe are inherited, but many genes are likely to contribute to this trait. As such, their pattern of inheritance is difficult to predict.

In this item, we try to give some genetic clues to help relate the genotype of certain genes to the likelihood of having the earlobe attached to the head or detached.
430.technical_description:
The degree of attachment of the earlobe from the ear with respect to the head is surely a polygenic feature (it depends on several genes) and it is not a characteristic that genetically responds to an "all or nothing", but rather there is a continuous distribution and there are degrees of adhesion of the lobe of the ear. Some people may have it completely detached, others totally attached, and in between there is a wide range of intermediate phenotypes (some people may have it more attached to the head and others more dettached). Studying this trait is important since in certain occasions can be associated to certain disorders in the development and with malformations (for example, defects in the musculature of the jaw and defects in the craniofacial morphology), since the genes that are associated with the type of earlobe that a person presents are also involved in the development.

To make this item we used the data published in a study that included 74660 individuals of different ethnicities or populations (Shaffer JR, et al., 2017); in this study 49 variants showed association with the earlobe type. In our test we study 3 of these variants, this 3 variants or SNPs are classified as significant in the global population. The general data of probability of having the earlobe attached or more attached than the average that we give in the results have been calculated taking into account the overall significance of these 3 SNPs, however, when we read this item we must take into account which ethnic group we belong to, since not for all the populations the degree of significance of each polymorphism is the same.

It has been described that the G allele of the rs3827760 or g.109513601A>G polymorphism within the EDAR gene is associated with a greater likelihood of having the earlobe attached. The frequency of this allele in the global population is 24%: in European individuals the frequency of this allele is 1%; in individuals from East Asia, the frequency is very high, 87%; in South Asian population the frequency of this allele is 1%; in Americans it is 39%; in Latin Americans approximately 40%; and in Africans it is approximately 0.3%. Therefore, it is expected that in Chinese and East Asian individuals there is a high percentage of individuals with the earlobe attached while in European-Americans and Africans this characteristic is uncommon and also, the presence of this polymorphism is described as non significant for the development of this trait (Shaffer JR, et al., 2017).

According to the study published by Shaffer JR. and colleagues, the presence of one G allele confers susceptibility to develop earlobe attachment or certain degree of attachment; however, functional studies in Edar protein knockout mice show that those mice that still having an intact copy of this protein (allele A) and only one copy of the EDAR gene, still have the earlobe detached, while mice with the two copies of the EDAR gene deleted present earlobes attached (Adhikari K. et al., 2015). These functional data seem to indicate that, regarding this polymorphism,  for an individual to present a visible effect on the degree of attachment, is required that the variant allele is presented in homozygosis. Edar protein participates during prenatal development in determining the location, size, and shape of different appendages of the body, such as hair follicles, teeth, and certain glands.

However, as we have described at the beginning of the item, the attachment of the earlobe depends on several genes, so the mere presence of a G allele, added to the presence of other alleles in other genes associated with this physical characteristic, adds a certain probability.

The contribution of each of the associated alleles will determine the greater or lesser degree of the protrusión angle of the earlobe.

Another polymorphism described as associated with earlobe attachment is the rs2244503 or g.64960196 A>G in the ADAMTS9-AS2 gene. The allele G adds probability for earlobe attachment in the general population. The frequency of this allele in the general population is 25%, in Africans is 40%, in Americans is 18%, in Europeans 21%, in individuals from East Asia the frequency is 3% and in South Asian individuals the allelic frequency is 38%. It is described that for Chinese individuals the presence of this mutation is not significant, that is, it seems that it is not associated with earlobe attachment.

Another polymorphism associated with this trait is the rs9333075 or c.343 + 516 C>T in the ITGA8 gene. The T allele adds probability for earlobe attachment in the general population. The frequency of the T allele in the global population is 45%, in Africans is 48%, in Americans, 49%, in East Asian individuals, is 61%, South Asian individuals have an allelic frequency for the T variant of 38% and for Europeans the frequency is 27%. It is described that in Latin American individuals and Chinese or East Asian individuals the presence of this polymorphism is not significant and does not seem associated with the presence of earlobe attachment.

The calculation we use to describe the probability of having the earlobe attached (or certain degree of attachment) is based on the significance of the polymorphisms described for the global population, all of them are significant for a global population. However, when studying this item you must take into account your population and determine if the polymorphisms that you present are significant within your ethnic group (if the polymorphisms or SNPs are described as significant to develop the trait for each population is explained in the description of each polymorphism in this technical report).
430.technical_citations:
Shaffer JR, Li J, Lee MK, Roosenboom J, Orlova E, Adhikari K, et al. Multiethnic GWAS Reveals Polygenic Architecture of Earlobe Attachment. Am J Hum Genet, 2017; 101(6):913–24.
Adhikari K, Reales G, Smith AJP, Konka E, Palmen J, Quinto-Sanchez M, et al. A genome-wide association study identifies multiple loci for variation in human ear morphology. Nat Commun, 2015; 6(1):7500.

432.name: Secretor status and ABH antigens (FUT2 gene)
432.description: ABH blood group and Lewis antigens are not only found on red blood cells, where A and B determine the group, but these molecules are also present in a wide variety of tissues and can trigger susceptibility to infections and contribute to the development of other thromboembolic and cardiovascular diseases.
432.overview:
The ABH blood group and Lewis antigens are not only found in red blood cells, where A and B determine the group, these molecules are also present, for example, in a wide variety of tissues and can trigger susceptibility to infection, immunity, and many thromboembolic and cardiovascular diseases.

The epithelia of the gastrointestinal, respiratory, urinary, and reproductive tracts express on their surfaces carbohydrates conjugated with lipids (glycolipids) and proteins (glycoproteins) that have in their structure the ABH and Lewis antigens.

The FUT2 gene encodes for an enzyme, known as α-1,2-fucosyltransferase, which is essential for the secretion of the soluble forms of the blood group ABH and Lewis antigens in the mucosa and secretory glands. These soluble antigens produce significant physiological changes in the tissue microenvironment, affecting bacterial adherence and immunoglobulin status, among other processes.

The polymorphisms that reduce enzymatic α-1,2-fucosyltransferase activity such as W143X in Caucasians and I129F in Asians, produce the "non-secretory" phenotype that is characterized by not having these antigens in the body's secretions and, consequently, affects their susceptibility to many pathogens and diseases.
432.technical_description:
The 1, 2-fucosyltransferase enzyme, encoded by the FUT2 gene, is a molecule involved in the synthesis of blood group ABH antigens, which are carbohydrates that are part of the glycocalyx on the surface of red blood cells. These antigens are not exclusive to red blood cells but are also present in lymphocytes, platelets, and epithelial cells of the mucosa.

A relatively high proportion of individuals are "secretors", which means that antigens that are present on the surface of red blood cells and other cells are also found in soluble form in body fluids such as saliva and mucus. Being "secretor" (Se) or "non-secretor" (se) is completely independent of whether the individual has a blood group A, B, AB, or O. Among the secretors, a person of blood group A secretes antigens A and H, if blood group is B secretes antigens B and H, and in the case of the O group secretes only H (Metgud et al., 2016).

The rs601338 genetic variant (p.Trp154Ter or W143X), encodes a stop codon and produces a non-functional form of the 1,2-fucosyltransferase that, as we have previously indicated, is necessary for the release of the soluble forms of these antigens in different body secretions. Carriers of two copies of the p.Trp154Ter mutation (genotype AA) have the inactivated form of the enzyme and are "non-secretors"(se) while people with GG and AG are "secretors"(Se) (Velkova et al., 2017).

The mutations that inactivate the presence of 1, 2-fucosyltransferase vary in different populations, it is estimated that about 20% of the Caucasian population is "non-secretor" and the causative variant is rs601338 while in Asians it is rs1047781 (c.418A>T or I129F). 

As mentioned before, the enzyme 1,2-fucosyltransferase is not only found in red blood cells but also participates in the production of mucin glycans that are expressed on the surfaces of the intestinal mucosa where they interact with the microbiota. Some variants of the FUT2 gene, including rs601338 (p.Trp154Ter), are thought to produce terminal fucose residues in the mucin glycans, which prevent adequate binding of the microbiota to the intestinal mucosa and could affect susceptibility to infection.

Many studies have suggested that mutations in the FUT2 gene may confer some protection against infection by some microorganisms such as Helicobacter pylori, Norovirus (for more information, see the report on Norovirus resistance, in Traits) and Rotavirus, while conversely, they may increase susceptibility to other pathogens such as Candida (Azab et al., 2018).

The non-secretory state, determined by genotype rs601338 AA, also appears to be associated with mean total vitamin B12 concentrations. Several GWAS studies in the Caucasian population have shown that there may be a strong relationship between the FUT2 gene and vitamin B12 concentration and have observed that people with the 'non-secretory' phenotype (genotype AA) have higher concentrations of circulating vitamin B12 than secretory ones. The study by Velkova et al. suggests that impairment of FUT2 activity due to this inactivating mutation may affect vitamin B12 recycling as explained in the report on vitamin B12 levels in the Wellness part.

Hazra A, Kraft P, Lazarus R, Chen C, Chanock SJ, Jacques P, Selhub J, Hunter DJ. Genome-wide significant predictors of metabolites in the one-carbon metabolism pathway. Hum Mol Genet. 2009;18(23):4677-87.

443.name: Blood coagulation, factor V Leiden and 20210G-A
443.description: Thrombophilias are hereditary and/or acquired pathologies that predispose to blood clots or thrombosis. Among the genetic factors identified, the Factor V Leiden mutation and the prothrombin gene 20210G-A mutation are the most studied.
443.overview:
Thrombophilias are hereditary and/or acquired conditions that predispose the individual to suffer thrombosis or blood clots. The term hereditary thrombophilia has been generally applied to pathologies in which a genetic mutation affects the amount or function of a protein in the coagulation system. The most studied mutations include factor V Leiden or rs6025 mutation and prothrombin gene 20210A-G mutation or PGM, and those altering the antithrombin 3 (AT3 or SERPINC1), protein C (PROC), protein S (PROS1) genes.

Hypercoagulability or thrombophilias are multifactorial conditions that can have a different etiology. In addition to the potential genetic contribution, several factors may be considered to assess properly the individual thrombophilia risk. Acquired thrombophilia can be due to specific diseases as e.g. myeloproliferative neoplasms and deficiencies in the natural coagulants. Certain drug treatments (oral contraceptives, hormone treatments, and chemotherapy) and other factors as smoking, obesity, and pregnancy can also increase the thrombosis risk and should be considered.

People more prone to blood hypercoagulation are at greater risk of developing deep venous thrombosis (DVT) that is characterized by a tendency to clot formation in leg veins. Although clots mainly are formed in the legs, they can be produced in other body parts as lungs leading to pulmonary embolism. Venous thrombosis is the main term that includes DVT and pulmonary embolism and its incidence is estimated in 1-2 per 1000 individuals every year, being the third most common cardiovascular disease.
443.technical_description:
Factor V Leiden

The R506Q, rs6025, or p.Arg534Gln mutation (previously known as p.Arg506Gln) in the F5 gene is commonly referred to as "factor V Leiden". R506Q was first identified in 1994 in the Dutch city of Leiden by Prof R. Bertina. Between 3% and 8% of Caucasians in the United States and Europe are carriers of a copy of the factor V Leiden mutation and it is rare in Asian, African, and indigenous Australian populations. Carriers of two copies are only 1 among 5,000 Europeans.

The Factor V Leiden or F5 molecule is encoded by the F5 gene. It is a plasma glycoprotein that circulates in the blood and in its natural state has little or no activity. Normally, when the coagulation system is not active the APC (activated Protein C) has an inactivating effect of Factor V Leiden preventing coagulation, in other words, APC act as an anticoagulant substance. When Factor V Leiden mutation is produced at the F5 gene the mutated factor V Leiden is more resistant to APC action, the inactivation rate is 10-fold slower than normal, resulting in increased thrombin generation. Thrombin is the activated prothrombin enzyme form that promotes blood coagulation; active thrombin converts the soluble fibrinogen to insoluble fibrin strands. Thus, higher thrombin levels could lead to an increased predisposition to abnormal clot formation.

It has been seen that the presence of Factor V Leiden mutation in the F5 gene is one of the main genetic factors that lead to venous thromboembolism (VTE), a disorder that includes deep vein thromboembolism and pulmonary embolisms. VTE is characterized by the abnormal formation of blood clots, which in this case affect mainly the legs' veins.

Homozygosity for the R506Q mutation (two copies of the mutation) increases the risk of thrombotic complications to a greater extent than heterozygosity (presence of one copy of the mutation). Furthermore, the existence of additional mutations in coagulation genes acquired thrombophilic disorders, and other risk factors should be considered to assess properly the whole individual thrombosis risk.

The study published in 2013, which was conducted on more than 11,000 VTE cases and 21,000 controls, showed that the rs6025 mutation increases VTE susceptibility by approximately four times when a copy of the mutation is present. For carriers of two copies (homozygotes) of the factor V Leiden the risk observed was 11 times greater than the observed risk in non-carriers, although these cases were relatively exceptional (Simone et al., 2013). Other findings suggested that the risk for venous thrombosis is 80-fold times higher in factor V Leiden homozygotes in comparison with individuals without this variant (Zhang et al., 2018).

20210G-A

The F2 gene encodes for prothrombin protein. Prothrombin is a glycoprotein produced by the liver and has a vital role in converting the fibrinogen to fibrin during clot formation to stop bleeding when an injure is produced.

The 20210G-A mutation, also known as rs1799963, c.*97G>A or F2 mutation is considered a thrombophilia risk factor. The 20210G-A mutation produces the substitution of a guanine by an adenine in the nucleotide position 20210 at the 3’ untranslated region of the prothrombin gene. It has been observed that carriers of one copy of the c.*97G>A variant and homozygous (two copies) for the mutation have an elevated prothrombin level of 30% and 70% above non-mutation carriers, respectively (Zhang et al., 2018).

The prothrombin mutation seems to increase the risk of arterial thrombosis combined with other risk factors at a young age. The 20210G-A frequency in Europe ranges from 0 to 4% and prevalence in North America is estimated from 1.6 to 2.4%. 20210G-A is very rare or practically absent in Asian, African, Amerindians, and Australians (Poudel et al., 2020).

Pregnancy and postpartum are associated with a higher predisposition (fivefold increased risk) of clot formation and decreased anticoagulant activity. It was suggested by many studies that 20210G-A together with factor V Leiden mutation are present in 40% and 17% of VTE cases during pregnancy and both mutations may have a role in pregnancy loss and preeclampsia. Nevertheless, the relationship between inherited thrombophilia and adverse pregnancy outcomes is still unclear, the findings remain controversial and most women with factor V Leiden and 20210G-A without a personal history of VTE have normal pregnancies and postpartum (Bradley et al., 2011). Testing of inherited thrombophilia may be recommended by the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists when women have a personal history of VTE or have a first-degree relative (e.g. parent or sibling) with history of inherited thrombophilia 

If you carry 20210G-A and/or factor V Leiden mutations only suggest an elevated VTE risk but cannot definitively predict the occurrence or recurrence of a VTE. As previously indicated, many factors determine the individual VTE risk and must be also evaluated.

OTHER GENETIC AND NON-GENETIC FACTORS

Other genetic factors that affect the risk of venous thrombosis are:

Protein C deficiency (pathogenic mutations in the PROC gene)
Protein S deficiency (pathogenic mutations in the PROS1 gene)
F8 gene mutations
SERPINE1 mutations

Concerning other factors that can raise the predisposition to blood hypercoagulation:

Age
Pregnancy and postpartum
Combined oral contraceptives
Hormone Replacement Therapy (HRT)
Treatment with Selective Estrogen Receptor Modulators (SERMs)
Obesity
Cancer (e.g. lung or pancreatic cancer) and other medical conditions as inflammatory bowel disease
Infections
Abnormally high levels of certain clotting proteins or low levels of proteins that prevent clots
Smoking
Sitting or lying in bed for long periods of time
Having a family history of thrombosis affecting at least one first-degree relative
Complication after central venous catheter insertion
