444.name: Blood Group ABO/Rh
444.description: The blood group of each individual is determined by the surface proteins expressed on the membrane of the red blood cells. Of the multitude of systems described, the most relevant, especially for human-to-human compatibility, are the ABO and Rh systems.
444.overview:
The blood group is one of the classification systems of human blood. In the erythrocytes or red blood cells there are adhered substances, the antigens, that define these blood groups and that are responsible for a donor and a recipient in a blood transfusion. In total there are more than 300 antigens that may or may not be on the surface of red blood cells and that are those that mark blood groups. There are more than 30 blood type classification groups, but the two most important are the ABO group and the Rh group because they affect the vast majority of the world's population.

People in group A have antigen A. People in group B have antigen B. People in group AB have both antigens and people in group O have neither. This is the ABO classification.

Likewise, in the Rh classification, Rh + people present the Rh antigen while Rh- do not.

These antigens found on the surface of red blood cells (A and B antigens for the ABO classification system; Rh antigen for the Rh classification system) react when they come into contact with blood that is of a different type and does not have the same antigens on the surface of the red blood cells. When this happens, the unsupported blood recipient makes antibodies against these different antigens of the donor. The presence or not of each type of antigen (For the ABO system: A, B, AB-presenting both antigens, O-presenting neither antigen A nor antigen B; For the Rh system: Rh + individual presents the Rh antigen, Rh – individual does not present the Rh antigen) is the factor that determines who can give blood to whom.
444.technical_description:
IMPORTANT: With this analysis we make a prediction of a person's blood type thanks to the genetic variants described for each blood group. The reliability of this technique is 90%, so there may be cases (10%) in which genetics do not match the blood group. Therefore, to know exactly your blood type, you must perform a blood biochemical analysis.

 

ABO Rh Blood Group

The molecular basis of the blood group antigens was identified first in the 1980s and 1990s. Since the time Karl Landsteiner discovered ABO blood groups, agglutination was the method of testing for detecting the presence of blood group antigens and antibodies.

However, during the last decades the genetics of the ABO Rh blood type has been detailed, and nowadays the blood type can be analyzed by genetic methods.

After studying the genes that are involved in the definition of the blood type carefully, it was observed that single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) is the main cause of variation in these genes. One or more SNPs in particular blood group system can help to identify specific alleles of that system. Apart from this, various other causes responsible for variation seen in different blood group alleles at the molecular level include deletion of a gene or an exon or a nucleotide(s).

The ABO gene is located on ‘q’ arm of chromosome 9 (9q34). It encodes a glycosyltransferase which catalyses the addition of a monosaccharide into a carbohydrate sequence expressing the H antigen. N-Acetyl-galactosamine is a specific sugar molecule responsible for expression of “A group”, whereas D-galactose is a specific sugar molecule responsible for “B group” expression.

A, B and AB individuals express glycosyltransferase activities converting the H antigen into A or B antigens, whereas O (H) individuals lack such activity.

The A and B groups differ in a few single-base substitutions in the ABO gene sequence, that cause the differences in A and B group type.

 

A critical single-base deletion was found in the O group, which results in an entirely different inactive protein incapable of modifying the H antigen.

 

Genotyping of Rh group: After ABO, Rh is the second clinically important blood group system. It is encoded by two genes, namely, RHD and RHCE, which are closely linked and highly homologous and located on chromosome 1, exact location being 1p36.1. Both these genes are inherited together. In this item we can predict the Rh+ or Rh- individuals by studying several SNPs located at RHD gene.

451.name: Gene MTHFR
451.description: The MTHFR gene codes for a key enzyme in folate metabolism. A large number of studies have associated the presence of common variants in the C677T and A1298C populations with a decreased folate metabolic capacity that could be related to several clinical conditions.
451.overview:
The MTHFR gene encodes an enzyme that converts folic acid (vitamin B9) into a form that can be used by the human body, and is also responsible for converting homocysteine to methionine.  Mutations in the MTHFR gene can lead to low blood levels of folate and high levels of homocysteine, which, in turn, can cause certain disorders.

The MTHFR enzyme catalyzes the reduction of 5,10-methylenetetrahydrofolate to the main circulating form of folate, 5-methyltetrahydrofolate, in an irreversible reaction. The products of the folate pathway are involved in the transformation of homocysteine to methionine, an essential precursor for DNA methylation and gene regulation. Mutations in the MTHFR gene can lead to dysregulation of homocysteine levels with consequent accumulation in the blood, resulting in different problems.

in this analysis, two common variants (C677T and A1298C) in the MTHFR gene coding for the enzyme methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase are analyzed. Both genetic variants are frequently analyzed and studied because of their potential association with folic acid processing efficiency and various conditions, including various types of cancer, coronary artery disease, altered plasma lipid levels, and neural tube defects.

However, many of the scientific findings remain inconsistent and inconclusive, so both variants are not tested during clinical practice.

210.name: Probability of having red hair
210.description: Red hair color is the result of the combination of two types of melanin pigments in specific amounts, which ultimately determines the characteristic orange color. In addition, this characteristic production of melanin pigments can also determine the color of the skin or even the eyes together.
210.overview:
Hair color is a result of pigmentation (coloration) due to the presence of two chemical substances of melanin: eumelanin and pheomelanin. Generally, when more melanin is present, the darker the hair color; when less, it is lighter.

Natural hair colors

Natural hair color is black, brown (chestnut), blond and red. Hair color is genetically associated with skin color and eye color. Black hair is the most common while blond and red hair is associated more frequently with genetic characteristics such as albinism, and with illness such as skin cancer.

Genetics and biochemistry of hair color

Two types of pigment give hair its color: eumelanin and pheomelanin. Eumelanin is black and brown while pheomelanin is red.

Higher levels of eumelanin in the hair determine how dark it is. For example, a lower concentration of brown eumelanin will cause the hair to be blonder and a higher eumelanin concentration will make it browner (a much higher amount of black eumelanin will result in black while a lesser amount will make it gray).

All humans have pheomelanin in their hair. Pheomelanin is more chemically stable than black eumelanin, but less chemically stable than brown eumelanin, so it breaks down more slowly when oxidized. This is why bleach gives darker hair a reddish tinge during the artificial coloring process. As the pheomelanin continues to break down, the hair will gradually become orange, then yellow, and finally white.

According to one theory, at least two gene pairs control human hair color.

    One phenotype (brown/blonde) has a dominant brown allele and a recessive blond allele.

Thus, a person with a brown allele will have brown hair; a person with no brown alleles will be blond.

This explains why two brown-haired parents can produce a blond-haired child.

    The other gene pair is a non-red/red pair, where the non-red allele (which suppresses production of pheomelanin) is dominant and the allele for red hair is recessive.

Thus, a person with two copies of the red-haired allele will have red hair but it could be reddish brown, auburn or a brilliant orangey-red, depending on if the first pair of genes is brown or blond, respectively.

Response difference in redheads to some medicines

Redheads have different reactions to some medicines, especially to analgesics and anesthesia. Additionally, the majority of red-haired women have a small mutation in an allele (MC1R gene) which causes a greater response to some analgesics (they need less to get the same effect) than the rest of redheads or persons with other hair colors.

Some NCBI (National Center for Biotechnology Information) researchers have found that people with red hair require greater amounts of certain types of analgesics. Other NCBI reserch publications concluded that because of their genetics, red-haired women require a lesser amount of an analgesic called Pentazocine than women with other hair colors or than men with any hair color.

Another study by NCBI demonstrated that red-haired women had a greater response to those analgesics for a certain pain than red-haired men. A follow-up study by the same group showed that red-haired people in general (both men and women) were much more sensitive to a type of analgesic called Morphine-6-glucuronide (M6G) than persons with other hair colors.
210.technical_description:
Studies have shown that red hair is more common in Irish, Dutch, Swedish and Icelandic populations, suggesting a northern European origin of the most common MC1R variants. But red hair is also occasionally found in other populations around the world.

The allele that provides the susceptibility for red hair is rs1805008 (T), as opposed to the wild type rs1805008 (C) allele. Therefore, homozygous individuals with TT genotype have a higher probability (13-20x) of having red hair. Whereas heterozygotes carrying a single rs1805008-T allele do not normally have red hair (only 5%) but are more likely to have offspring with red hair if their partner is also a carrier. Approximately 70% of people with two variants in the MCR1 gene have red hair.

The identification of an association between human genetic variants of melanocortin receptor 1 (MC1R) with having red hair and white skin, as well as the relationship between variants of this gene and hair colour in animals, suggests that the MC1R gene is an integral control point in the normal pigmentation phenotype.

The study referred to was carried out in an Irish population, among which there is a preponderance of individuals with the light skin type. Seventy-five percent of the individuals contained one variant in the MC1R gene, and 30% of the individuals had both variants. The Arg151Cys, Arg160Trp and Asp294His variants were significantly associated with red hair. Importantly, there are no individuals who harboured two of these three variants and did not have red hair, although some redheads showed only one of the possible variants. The same three variants were also over-represented in individuals with fair skin type.

Despite these associations many subjects with dark hair/darker skin type harboured MC1R variants, but no evidence was detected for any particular association of variants in MC1R with the darker hair and skin phenotype.

The Asp294His variant was similarly associated with red hair in a Dutch population but was rare in red-haired subjects in Sweden. The Asp294His variant was also significantly associated with non-melanoma skin cancer in a UK population. The results show that the Arg151Cys, Arg160Trp and Asp294His variants are of key importance in determining the pigmentation phenotype and response to ultraviolet radiation and suggest that in many cases the red hair component and in some cases the light skin type is inherited as a recessive Mendelian inheritance pattern.

The rs1805007 (c.451C>T) polymorphism located in the MC1R gene is associated with red hair colour. People with one copy of the T allele have a certain probability of having red hair (although the presence of only one T allele is not a certainty that a person has red hair). Those with two copies of the T allele (TT genotype) have an approximately 70% chance of having red hair.

The rs1805009 polymorphism is located in the same gene as the rest of the polymorphisms studied associated with red hair colour (MCR1 gene). In relation to the different studies linking this SNP, it has been determined that individuals homozygous for the C allele in rs1805009 (CC genotype) are more likely to have red hair. The presence of a rs1805009-C allele implies the possibility of transmission of red hair colour to offspring if the partner is also a carrier.

The rs11547464, known as Arg142His, is one of several SNPs in the MC1R gene associated with red hair colour (redheads). The allele that confers a predisposition for red hair is rs11547464(A), compared to the wild-type allele rs11547464(G).

The rs1805008, known as Arg160Trp or R160W, is also one of several SNPs in the MC1R gene associated with red hair colour.

428.name: Asparagus odor detection
428.description: After ingesting asparagus, some people can detect an unmistakable aroma in their urine. Not all people have this ability, which is influenced by genetics.
428.overview:
The skin of the nose contains a lot of nerve cells covered with molecular sensors, called olfactory receptors. These receptors are specialized in detecting odor molecules. Using different combinations of more than 300 olfactory receptors, humans can detect thousands of different scents. The types of olfactory receptors determine what odors you can or cannot smell.

When humans eat asparagus, some people report a distinct odor afterward from their urine. However, the recognition of the asparagus odor in urine is not an all-or-nothing phenomenon; some people produce an asparagus odor that is easy to detect, and the presumption is that some people produce more odorant. Odorant production varies from individual to individual, and people with urine that does not have a detectable odor may produce it, albeit at a low concentration. Part of the difference in odorant production could also be due to the production by some people of less volatile variants. Also, some individuals are unable to detect asparagus odor.

Therefore, the most likely explanation is that these individual differences in odor detection are specific anosmia (loss of odor sense). Specific anosmias are common for biologically important odors, such as volatile steroid hormones, musk, and sweat, and the smell of human urine in different nutritional states, for example, after asparagus consumption.

One or more olfactory receptors respond to the asparagus odor but these receptors are less functional in some people. Differences in olfactory ability can be due to heritable variation in olfactory receptors. Although specific anosmias are often thought of as being all-or-none traits, thresholds are on a continuum and that is likely to be the case here. Some people are more sensitive than others, detecting the asparagus as if it were in higher concentrations than those normally found in human urine and, conversely,  other people are almost unable to detect it.
428.technical_description:
After the intake of asparagus, most people produce chemical compounds that provide an asparagus-related odor in urine. However, the detection of this smell in urine does not occur with the same intensity in all individuals. It seems that the ability to detect the smell of asparagus in urine after having ingested this vegetable is associated with the presence of a polymorphism or genetic variant in the OR2M7 gene, which is a gene that codes for an specific olfactory receptor. The variant or polymorphism is the g.248333561 G> A or rs4481887. Individuals with GG genotype have the least capacity to detect the odor of asparagus in the urine, individuals with GA genotype have greater capacity than GG individuals, and individuals with AA genotype are those with the greater capacity to detect the smell to asparagus in urine.

This result is important because informs us about possible anosmia, that is, a specific loss of sense of odor due to loss of function in one or more olfactory receptors.

431.name: Nasion prominence
431.description: Facial morphology is one of the most complex and variable features of each individual that determine part of the physical identity of the face. Part of this physical identity is determined by the degree of prominence of the nasion, the bony fusion point of the nasal bones and the frontal bone of each person.
431.overview:
Some people have the nasion more prominent than others. The nasion is the deepest point of the nasal bridge; it is the point of intersection of the frontal bone and two nasal bones of the human cranium. Its manifestation on the visible surface of the face is a notoriously depressed area that lies between the eyes, just above the bridge of the nose.

Some people have this area more prominent (exit outward) than others, and it seems that this facial feature is associated with certain genetic factors.

The facial characteristic that we study in this item is part of the set of craniofacial characteristics that seem to be related to the presence of certain polymorphisms in certain genes that are involved in the ontogenetic development of the human being. Furthermore, it has been described that certain polymorphisms associated with certain craniofacial characteristics are also associated with malformations, syndromes and developmental disorders.
431.technical_description:
A GWAS study ("Genome-Wide Association Study") conducted with 2,185 adolescents from the United Kingdom found a strong association between one polymorphism within the PAX3 gene and the prominence of the nasion (deepest point of the nasal bridge). The polymorphism is the rs7559271 or c.1174-1377 A>G. In this GWAS study conducted by Paternoster L. et al. in 2012, was described that each copy of a G allele is associated in men with an increase of 0.39 mm in the distance between the nasion and the middle endocantion (distance between the two internal angles of the eyes)*.

Thus, those individuals with the AG genotype will have a greater prominence of the nasion than the AA individuals, and those individuals with the GG genotype will have greater prominence than the AG.

In this study, in addition to observing the relationship of this polymorphism with the prominence of the nasion, it was also observed that this rs was associated with the height of the nasion in men.

The association of this polymorphism with this facial feature is also described in the work of Liu F. and collaborators of 2012.

*Note: The endocantion is the inner point of the commissure of the eyes near the lacrimal. There is a left endocantion and a right endocantion, and the middle endocantion is half the distance between the two endocantions.

The PAX3 gene is involved in certain craniofacial characteristics, and it has been shown that certain variants in this gene can produce craniofacial abnormalities associated with certain severe syndromes, such as Waardenburg Syndrome. But to date, the presence of the rs7559271 polymorphism has not been associated with any malformation.</p
431.technical_citations:
Paternoster L, Zhurov AI, Toma AM, Kemp JP, St Pourcain B, Timpson NJ, et al. Genome-wide association study of three-dimensional facial morphology identifies a variant in PAX3 associated with nasion position. Am J Hum Genet, 2012; 90(3):478–85.
Liu F, van der Lijn F, Schurmann C, Zhu G, Chakravarty MM, Hysi PG, et al. A genome-wide association study identifies five loci influencing facial morphology in Europeans. Gibson G, editor. PLoS Genet, 2012; 8(9): e1002932.

427.name: Earwax type / Armpit odor
427.description: The intensity of axillary odor and the type of cerumen that people have are interrelated traits, since they share elements between them, such as their transporter in the membranes of the cells where these traits are manifested.
427.overview:
Earwax contributes to our ears remain clean of microorganisms. It traps dirt and bacteria and slowly moves it up and out of the ear canal. Earwax also contains at least 10 compounds that help prevent bacteria from growing inside the ear.

Wet earwax is dark-colored and sticky, while dry earwax is light-colored and flaky. Both types are equally good at keeping dirt and bacteria at bay, but the difference between the two is determined by some variants in certain genes

Dry earwax is found in 80-95% of people of East Asian descent but in less than 3% of people of European or African descent.

One of the genes that are involved in earwax type production, is also involved in sweat production and thus in body odor. The same genetic variant in the ABCC11 gene that determines the dry earwax type is also linked to lower levels of body odor.
427.technical_description:
The earwax type secreted by each individual can be of different types and these differences can be seen within individuals of the same population and especially between the different ethnic groups or populations. Consistent with the differences in earwax type between populations, these differences in the texture and color are associated with the presence of an SNP in the ABCC11 gene. The polymorphism is the rs17822931 or c.538 C>T and it has been seen that those individuals with TT genotype possess a drier, almost colorless, and flaky earwax than individuals with CT or CC, which present a more humid, dark-colored and sticky earwax. Humid earwax is more prevalent in Europeans and Africans due to the high frequency of the C allele in these populations, while dry earwax is highly prevalent in Asian populations due to the high frequency of the T allele. This association has been confirmed by several studies, such as those by Yoshiura K et al. in 2006, Prokop-Prigge KA et al. in 2015, and Ohashi J et al. in 2011.

Coming back to the rs17822931 polymorphism in the ABCC11 gene, there are studies in which it has been seen that this variation not only affects the type of earwax but also the odor of the cerumen. Thus, a TT genotype determines cerumen that is dry and also almost odorless, while the CC and CT genotypes correspond to wet and odorous cerumen. Prokop-Prigge KA and collaborators carried out a study in 2015 in which they tried to see the association between the polymorphism of the rs17822931 genotype, the odour of earwax, and ancestry. They found that individuals of African and European ancestry who participated in the study had CC genotype and emitted a greater amount of volatile substances from cerumen (volatile aromatic substances were measured by gas chromatography), while individuals of Asian ancestry, most of them with a TT genotype, emitted less volatile substances.

In addition, this same polymorphism has been associated with greater or lesser body odor coming from axillary odour (Martin A et al 2010, Harker M et al 2014, Nakano M et al., 2009). The presence of a C allele is necessary and sufficient to secrete human-specific odorant conjugates that lead to axillary odor production. Thus, individuals with TT genotype for rs17822931 have dry earwax, odor is almost non-existent and produce a lower body odor by secretion through the axillary glands, while individuals with CC and CT genotypes present a humid earwax containing a greater amount of aromatic substances and also producing a greater body odor through the secretions of the axillary glands.

In addition, a study carried out by Rodríguez S. and collaborators in 2013 went further and correlated the rs17822931 genotype with the hygiene habits of individuals belonging to a large cohort of children and parents of the "Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children, ALSPAC "(17,000 individuals). Individuals who were homozygous TT were 5 times more represented among individuals who reported not using deodorant or who used it infrequently than among individuals who reported using deodorants regularly. It seems that genetics also determines the pattern of hygiene that people follow, this study has shown an associated behavior.

The ABCC11 gene codes for a cellular transporter that is found in the membranes of various cell types and is involved in the transport of small molecules and also of external agents (for example a large variety of drugs). The ABCC1 transporter is found in the membranes of the cells that secrete cerumen in the ear as well as in the cells of the glands that secrete sweat in the axilla, hence the relationship that exists between the presence of polymorphisms in this gene, type of earwax and type of sweat.

203.name: Duffy Antigen, malaria resistant
203.description: The Duffy antigen consists of a glycoprotein on the surface of erythrocytes that acts as a key mediator for the access of the Plasmodium vivax parasite to the interior of red blood cells during its life cycle. Thus, individuals who do not carry this antigen would be resistant to the forms of malaria caused by this species.
203.overview:
Each year malaria affects at least 350 million persons and causes the death of more than million, principally children and young people. Since the parasites that cause it can only transmit the disease from person to person by mosquito bites, malaria is usually most prevalent in the tropical and subtropical zones of the world where these insects flourish. When a malaria -bearing mosquito bites a person, it injects the parasites that it carries and these infect the red blood cells of the victim and multiply. One species of the malaria parasite, Plasmodium vivax, takes advantage of a protein that is on the surface of the red blood cells, the Duffy antigen, to get into the cells. Some people, referred to as Duffy negative, do not have the Duffy antigen in their red blood cells and consequently are resistant to the Plasmodium vivax malaria infection. Although this is the most common form of malaria, there are other forms that also infect humans and Duffy negative persons can be infected by them.

The Duffy antigen is also known as DARC, Duffy Antigen/Receptor for chemokines (or cytokines). Chemokines are proteins that act as signaling proteins of the cells that move in the body. Cells with receptors such as the Duffy antigen can detect and respond to the signals sent by the chemokines.

The version "C" of the SNP in the DARC gen prevents the creation of the protein taking place in the red blood cells. The Duffy antigen is also in other sells of the body and scientists are not yet sure what the Duffy antigen normally does in the body as a chemokine receptor. Their theory is that whatever its function it must not be very important in the red blood cells as many people are Duffy negative and are apparently healthy. On the other hand, the fact that in this version of this SNP the Duffy antigen is missing in the red blood cells only and not in other tissues suggests that the Duffy antigen could be important in other parts of the body.

Ths discovery of the Duffy antigen was crucial to understanding how P. vivax is able to infect the bodys red blood cells. Scientists used their knowledge about the Duffy antigen to find its similar protein in P. vivax. Researchers are trying to find the way to make vaccines act directly against this protein in particular of the parasite and so, in time, be able to provide protection against P. vivax to those persons who are not Duffy negative.
203.technical_description:
The rs2814778 (c.-67T> C) polymorphism is found in the DARC gene, which encodes the called Duffy blood group antigen. The presence or absence of the Duffy antigen in red blood cells (erythrocytes) is highly inheritable. If two copies of the C allele of this SNP or polymorphism are presented, the individual lacks Duffy antigen in the red blood cells and these individuals are known as Duffy negative, which means that they are resistant to Plasmadium vivax (one of the microorganisms that transmit malaria). In addition, other variants than the one reported in this technical report may also result in being Duffy-negative. It is important to keep in mind that resistance to the P. vivax microorganism does not make someone resistant to other forms of malaria. Travelers to areas where malaria is common should take all recommended precautions, regardless of their results for this SNP. This SNP shows a very high difference between the frequency of the C allele in Europeans (1%) and the frequency of the C allele in Africans (96%).

In conclusion, rs2814778-CC genotype protects against malaria by Plasmodium vivax and predicts the count of white blood cells and neutrophils

506.name: Gene MTR
506.description: Folate and homocysteine are involved in DNA synthesis and methylation processes. The MTR gene is one of the key enzymes involved.

246.name: Persistence of fetal hemoglobin
246.description: It has been demonstrated that the persistence of fetal hemoglobin has a marked hereditary character. There are several studies that relate certain genetic polymorphisms or variants with the persistence of fetal hemoglobin in adults and its possible implication in the improvement of symptoms of different types of anemia.
246.overview:
Fetal hemoglobin is the main oxygen transport protein in the human fetus during the last seven months of development in the uterus and persists in the newborn until roughly 6 months old, moment in which it is substituted by adult hemoglobin.

Functionally, fetal hemoglobin differs most from adult hemoglobin in that it is able to bind oxygen with greater affinity than the adult form, giving the developing fetus better access to oxygen from the mother´s bloodstream.

In some cases, the rate of said hemoglobin over the years does not diminish but rather maintains its uninterrupted synthesis in adulthood. In healthy patients, harmful effects or anemia have not been detected, even when the hemoglobin produced is fetal.

At present, research is being carried out to find ways to activate fetal hemoglobin to combat sickle-cell anemia or B- thalassemias which can cause symptoms such as:

    Breathing difficulty
    Dizziness
    Head ache
    Sensation of cold in hands and feet
    Abnormal paleness in skin or mucus membranes
    Jaundice (yellowing of the skin and whites of the eyes)
246.technical_description:
It is scientifically demonstrated that individuals who continue producing fetal hemoglobin (HbF) and therefore have a certain level of this type of plasma hemoglobin, have less severe symptoms if they suffer from different types of anemia, such as sickle cell anemia or β-thalassemia. In addition, it has been shown that the persistence of fetal hemoglobin has a marked hereditary character. There are several studies that relate certain genetic polymorphisms or genetic variants with the persistence of fetal hemoglobin (HbF) in adults and its possible involvement in the improvement of painful symptoms of different types of anemia.

Scientific studies have determined that adult individuals carrying at least one copy of the A allele of the rs10195871 polymorphism can continue producing some fetal hemoglobin so that their levels are higher, a fact that can reduce the severity of sickle cell anemia or thalassemia, since fetal hemoglobin can compensate the low levels of adult hemoglobin. The rs10195871 polymorphism is found in the BCL11A gene, which is expressed highly in different blood cell lines and is involved in changing the expression of the hemoglobin types during the transition of fetal to adult blood cells

426.name: Tooth morphology
426.description: Dental morphology is highly differentiated among human populations and research indicates that it may be influenced by genetic factors.
426.overview:
Dental morphology is highly differentiated among human populations and some researchers have indicated that dental morphology is influenced by genetic factors. In Asian and Asia-derived populations are common several dental features such as upper first and second incisors shovel-shaped and not aligned with the other teeth, upper first premolars with one root and lower first molars with three roots.

Tooth-shovelling was the dental feature most studied and is frequent in Asia, with a south-to north increasing cline, but rare or absent in Africa and Europe. However, other dental traits such as crown size and presence and number of molar cusps seems to be related to some genetic variations in loci that participate in the developmental pathways of teeth, glands, hair and other ectoderm related organs.
426.technical_description:
In 2009, Kimura et al. published a study to corroborate that the gene EDAR is strongly related to tooth morphology. The presence of the nonsynonymous polymorphism rs3827760, located in an exonic region of the EDAR (ectodysplasin A receptor) gene was highly associated with the tooth shoveling of incisors and crown size, particularly maximal diameter in anterior teeth. Heritability of tooth shoveling was around 0.75 in Asian and Native American and is rare among African and European populations. EDAR gene is involved in the pathway that regulates the development of the organs that have an ectodermal origin, such as hair, glands, nails, and teeth. Variants in the EDAR gene have been related to abnormalities during ectodermal-derived organ/structures development causing syndromes such as hypohidrotic ectodermal dysplasia.

Kimura et al. found that persons that are heterozygotes for rs3827760 AG had a higher tooth shoveling grade than persons with the AA genotype and homozygous rs3827760 GG have higher tooth shoveling grade than heterozygous rs3827760 AG. Furthermore, several studies suggest that the rs3827760-G allele is associated with the thicker hair cross-section observed in the Asian population (Thai-Mai, Indonesian, and Japanese) in comparison to European and African populations where the rs3827760 G allele show a very low frequency, less than 1% (Fujimoto et al., 2008).
426.technical_citations:
Fujimoto A, Ohashi J, Nishida N, Miyagawa T, Morishita Y, Tsunoda T, Kimura R, Tokunaga K. A replication study confirmed the EDAR gene to be a major contributor to population differentiation regarding head hair thickness in Asia. Hum Genet. 2008 Sep;124(2):179-85.

Kimura R, Yamaguchi T, Takeda M, Kondo O, Toma T, Haneji K, et al. A Common Variation in EDAR Is a Genetic Determinant of Shovel-Shaped Incisors. Am. J. Hum. Genet. 2009 Oct;85(4):528–35.

253.name: Photic sneeze reflex
253.description: The explanation why some people tend to sneeze when exposed to bright light remains an enigma. There are indications that certain variants may increase vulnerability to this particular trait
253.overview:
The sneeze reflex to a bright light is known as photic sneeze reflex. The persons exposed to a bright light (looking at the sun, coming out of a tunnel while driving, etc.) respond involuntarily by sneezing. Once the eyes become used to the light, the sneezing stops.

This reflex can be found in one of every four persons and occasionally is hereditary. The exact cause of this reflex is not known but it is thought to be an electric process in which the light stimulates the various nerves which are linked to the sneeze reflex.

The only way for persons who suffer from this reflex to prevent photic sneezing is to avoid looking directly at the sun or direct exposure to the sun by wearing sunglasses or to avoid sudden exposure to bright light.
253.technical_description:
Photic sneezing is a response mechanism caused by the reflection of bright light. When the light strikes our sensory receptors, certain people respond by sneezing.

One of the genetic polymorphisms or genetic variants that has been associated with said trait is rs10427255. Regarding this genetic variant, it has been observed that individuals who present two copies of the C allele (CC genotype) are more likely to sneeze as a mechanism of response to exposure to sunlight. In addition, it seems that individuals with TT genotype have a lower tendency to photonic sneezing

433.name: CCR5Delta32 and susceptibility to HIV infection
433.description: CCR5 acts as an important co-receptor used by HIV for cellular entry into TCD4+ lymphocytes. The presence of a natural 32 base pair deletion in the CCR5 gene, known as CCR5Delta32, produces a truncated receptor that is not expressed on the surface of lymphocytes, thus preventing viral access and conferring resistance to infection.
433.overview:
The human immunodeficiency virus or HIV is the cause of AIDS or Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome, which is characterized by a progressive weakening of the immune system leading to an increased risk of infection. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), HIV remains one of the biggest public health problems in the world, with 38 million people infected by the end of 2019.

It has been observed that some people are more susceptible than others to HIV infection and this may be due to the amount of CCR5, a transmembrane protein found on the surface of immune system cells and modulates its activation.

CCR5 acts as a receptor and interacts with other molecules, triggering a signalling cascade that mainly activates T cells or T lymphocytes, of the CD4+ and CD8+ types. CCR5 binds to chemokines, which are small molecules that are released into the extracellular media and are responsible for directing lymphocytes towards tissues that are inflamed, infected or where an injury has occurred.

The discovery in 1996 that CCR5 was key to the entry of HIV into lymphocytes along with the CD4 receptor was a significant breakthrough in the fight against AIDS. In the same year, a mutation, known as CCR5Delta32, was shown to exist in the CCR5 gene, dramatically reducing the amount of the CCR5 receptor in lymphocytes membrane and demonstrating that it could be a crucial protective factor against HIV infection.

It was not until 2008 that CCR5 became of great public interest. Gero Hütter announced that an HIV-positive patient, known as "the Berlin patient", had been transplanted with bone marrow from a donor with two copies of the CCR5Delta32 mutation and became HIV-negative even though he was off antiretroviral treatment.

The relationship of CCR5Delta32 to infection with various pathogens and the role of CCR5 in the immune response continue to be studied and potential AIDS treatments and therapies developed.
433.technical_description:
The human C-C chemokine receptor type 5 (CCR5) is a molecule that is in the cell surface (is a seven transmembrane alpha-helices protein) and interacts with chemokines. CCR5 acts as a receptor for several specific chemokines (CCL3, CCL4, and CCL5) that forms part of a small signaling protein family that are secreted by cells.

Chemokines are involved in many biological processes as guiding the immune cells in response to infections, blood cell production, and outgrowth of new capillary blood vessels from existing blood vessels.

In the immune cells, like leukocytes, the chemokine-CCR5 interaction mediates its migration to the infected tissue. This interaction also regulates other types of immune cells, including natural killer and regulatory T cells. Due to the importance of CCR5 function in immune cell migration and inflammatory processes the CCR5Delta32 variant has aroused great interest for scientists since it avoids the location of the CCR5 receptor in the plasma membrane by the production of a defective truncated receptor that is not placed on the cell surface and therefore impairs the interaction of the chemokines with the receptor.

The CCR5Delta32 (rs333) genetic variant is a 32 base-pair deletion in the CCR5 gene that was firstly discovered in 1996 as a strong candidate for HIV-1 (human immunodeficiency virus 1). HIV-1 is an RNA-based virus whose genetic material is surrounded by nucleocapsid proteins. Its entry into CD4+ immune T cells occurs through interaction with CCR5 and CD4+ receptors (Ellwanger et al., 2020).

If an individual carries one copy of the CCR5Delta32 deletion has decreased CCR5 levels in the cell surface, CCR5 receptors appear located in the cellular membrane because is still being available another CCR5 gene copy without the deletion which produces the common or wildtype CCR5 receptor. It has been shown that heterozygous or carriers of one CCR5Delta32 copy have certain protection against HIV infection due to the reduced CCR5 membrane levels. In contrast, if an individual carries two alleles of CCR5Delta32 it is notably more resistant to HIV. Although the protection is high in two Delta32 allele carriers it continues not being total and some infected CCR5Delta32 homozygotes have been described in the scientific literature (Parmentier, 2015).

Hematopoietic stem-cell transplantation using cells from a donor with two copies of rs333 (CCR5Delta32/Delta32) is emerging as a promising therapy for HIV patients with possibilities of reducing notably the use of antiviral drugs and hence its side effects (Gupta et al., 2019).

The CCR5 receptor is not used only by HIV-1 strain, other circulating virus strains are able to enter CD4+ T cells through CCR5-interaction. Many researches have been conducted in the last years to find out the impact of CCR5Delta32 variant in many viruses infection as the West Nile virus, Human cytomegalovirus, Crimean-Congo haemorrhagic fever virus, Influenza viruses, Enterovirus, Poliovirus, Dengue virus and more recently Zika Virus, Epstein-Barr virus, and Rhinovirus. CCR5Delta32 possible influence on SARS-CoV-2 clinical course after the infection is also under research (Ellwanger et al., 2020). For instance, it has been found that although individuals homozygous for CCR5Delta32 (who have two copies) have some protection against HIV, they may be more likely to be infected with the West Nile virus, among others (Vangelista and Vento, 2018).

Recently, it has been proposed that the CCR5 receptor may have another function in the immune response. It has been seen in an experimental vaccination model that when CCR5 is activated by antigen exposure it contributes to the aggregation of the T-lymphocyte membrane receptors (TCR or "T-cell antigen receptor") in the form of nanoclusters that promote the reactivation of CD4+ T-lymphocytes (Matti y Legler, 2020).

The formation of nanoclusters could reinforce the effect of a new contact of the CD4+ antigen with the T-cells, which activates the T-cells at very low doses of antigen, thus acting as a "long-term protection" mechanism. This finding suggests that individuals with CCR5 deficiency, due to the presence of CCR5Delta32 or other CCR5 mutations, may be more susceptible to having an altered immune response and that when they are vaccinated against viruses (such as influenza) their immunization is decreased. However, more studies are needed to confirm this hypothesis in humans (Matti and Legler, 2020).

507.name: Gene MTRR
507.description: The MTRR gene encodes for methionine synthase reductase, an enzyme of the folate cycle that together with MTHFR participates in the reaction of methionine formation from homocysteine.

450.name: HLA-B27 antigen
450.description: Human leukocyte antigens (HLA) are proteins that help the body's immune system differentiate between its own cells and foreign, harmful substances. The HLA-B27 protein, located on the surface of white blood cells, has been linked to the development of spondyloarthropathies, a group of inflammatory diseases including ankylosing spondylitis.
450.overview:
The major histocompatibility complex (MHC) present in all vertebrates is known as the human leukocyte antigen system or HLA. The molecules that constitute the MHC are found on the surface of the cells that make up our tissues and are encoded by genes located on chromosome 6. MHCs are the most polymorphic proteins and are involved in the presentation of antigens to a type of immune cell known as cytotoxic T cells. MHC binds to fragments of pathogen-derived proteins and displays these peptides on its surface to cytotoxic T cells and activates the immune system response. 

We have more than 200 genes coding for MHC proteins (HLA in humans) that can be divided into two groups: class I and class II. There are three main types of MHC class I molecules called A, B, and C that are expressed in most cells in the body. B27 is one of the most common B alleles in Europeans. The rs4349859 single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) genotype is considered a genetic marker of HLA-B27-positive individuals of Caucasian ancestry; it is not correlated in African or Asian populations.

The HLA-B27 test is a serological test to detect the HLA-B27 protein located on the surface of white blood cells. The presence of the HLA-B27 antigen is associated with the development of spondyloarthropathies, a group of inflammatory diseases that typically affect the axial skeleton and include, among others, ankylosing spondylitis (AS). Together with rheumatoid arthritis, they are the two most common rheumatic-type diseases in the general population, with a prevalence of AS for the European population of 0.23%.

In addition, the presence of HLA-B27 is related to the appearance of other pathologies, mainly autoimmune disorders such as reactive arthritis, psoriatic arthropathy, juvenile rheumatoid arthritis, and enteropathic arthritis.

Therefore, early detection of HLA-B27-positive patients is of paramount importance, as this will make it possible to take precautions aimed at prevention and/or to apply more effective and earlier treatments.

243.name: Smell
243.description: People can discern thousands of odors, although there is great variability among people in the ability to detect particular odors. Some of the differences in this detection ability are believed to be genetically determined.
243.overview:
Our sense of smell—like your sense of taste—is part of your chemosensory system, or the chemical senses.

Your ability to smell comes from specialized sensory cells, called olfactory sensory neurons, which are found in a small patch of tissue high inside the nose. These cells connect directly to the brain. Each olfactory neuron has one odor receptor. Microscopic molecules released by substances around us—whether it’s coffee brewing or pine trees in a forest—stimulate these receptors. Once the neurons detect the molecules, they send messages to your brain, which identifies the smell. There are more smells in the environment than there are receptors, and any given molecule may stimulate a combination of receptors, creating a unique representation in the brain. These representations are registered by the brain as a particular smell.

Smells reach the olfactory sensory neurons through two pathways. The first pathway is through your nostrils. The second pathway is through a channel that connects the roof of the throat to the nose. Chewing food releases aromas that access the olfactory sensory neurons through the second channel. If the channel is blocked, such as when your nose is stuffed up by a cold or flu, odors can’t reach the sensory cells that are stimulated by smells. As a result, you lose much of your ability to enjoy a food’s flavor. In this way, your senses of smell and taste work closely together.

Without the olfactory sensory neurons, familiar flavors such as chocolate or oranges would be hard to distinguish. Without smell, foods tend to taste bland and have little or no flavor. Some people who go to the doctor because they think they’ve lost their sense of taste are surprised to learn that they’ve lost their sense of smell instead.

Your sense of smell is also influenced by something called the common chemical sense. This sense involves thousands of nerve endings, especially on the moist surfaces of the eyes, nose, mouth, and throat. These nerve endings help you sense irritating substances—such as the tear-inducing power of an onion—or the refreshing coolness of menthol.

It is believed that the human being is capable of distinguishing up to 10,000 different odors, but the majority of us perceive only a fraction of these in our lifetime. The appreciation of an aroma is very subjective an can be influenced by particular cultural or emotional aspects. That is why the description of an odor can vary from one person to another.
243.technical_description:
People can detect thousands of odors, although there is great variability among people in the ability to detect particular odors. Some of the differences in this detection ability are believed to be genetically determined.

The rs6591536 polymorphism (c.547G>A) is an SNP located in the OR5A1 olfactory receptor gene and has been associated with the ability to detect a certain type of aroma.

The carriers of one or two G alleles in this polymorphism have more capacity to detect a particular compound, the β-ionone ("floral smell", used in perfumery and as a flavoring in food). Individuals with AG or GG genotypes are more prone to prefer fragrances and household products that contain β-ionone, compared to individuals who present two copies of the A allele (genotype AA), who do not usually perceive such smell and its associated aromatic characteristics.

Studies have shown that this relationship between genotype and smell detection was observed in 96% of the cases

201.name: Alcohol flush reaction
201.description: Alcohol flush reaction is a type of intolerance related to the ability to metabolize alcohol. It is a disorder primarily caused by inherited genetic variations in the genes of certain enzymes, which leads to less efficient alcohol metabolism in individuals. This reaction manifests itself mainly as facial redness or flushing, among other symptoms, hence it is also known as alcohol flush reaction.
201.overview:
The reaction to alcohol, known as "alcohol flush", is caused by a disorder in the hepatic enzyme aldehyde dehydrogenase (ALDH2) impeding it to adequately metabolize acetaldehyde.

When alcohol metabolizes in the liver it first oxidizes to acetaldehyde and then it is converted into acetate by ALDH2. The persons who have a certain facial flush after drinking a small quantity of alcohol have a genetic change in their ALDH2 which causes the body to metabolize alcohol more quickly, but the decomposition of acetaldehyde is less efficient. Acetaldehyde accumulation makes the blood vessels dilate and the skin turns reddish. This reaction happens especially in the Asian populations and is also known as the "Asian flush" or "Asian glow".

Recent studies have shown that those individuals with an ALDH2 deficiency have also been associated with a greater risk for esophageal cancer and high blood pressure. Acetaldehyde is a byproduct of alcohol metabolism but it is also a carcinogen and mutagen with recognized enabling properties directly related to cancer. When the digestive tract is repeatedly exposed to acetaldehyde, the probability of damage in the DNA and of mutation can increase.

There is a very clear distinction between an alcohol flush and alcohol allergy. The flush reaction is not an allergic reaction although the symptoms can seem similar. The reasons for each reaction are quite different and therefore should be treated differently.

An allergy is an erroneous reaction committed by our body when faced by apparently innocuous substances which the body perceives as prejudicial.

The flush reaction to alcohol is an incapacity of the body to metabolize alcohol correctly because of a ALDH2 enzyme deficiency, i.e., it is not capable of breaking down the byproduct of alcohol metabolism, a chemical called acetaldehyde. This byproduct is toxic and an accumulation of it in the organism can provoke the characteristic symptom of alcohol flush.
201.symptoms: The symptoms include a reddish facial color or flushing, heart palpitations, dry mouth and throat which can be accompanied by nauseas and headaches.
201.prevention: The best way to prevent the flushing reaction is to avoid drinking alcohol.
201.technical_description:
The rs671 is a classic SNP related with the phenomena known as "Asian Flush".

The rs671 allele (A) of the ALDH2 gene is the responsible of this phenomena, because encodes a form of the aldehyde dehydrogenase 2 protein that is defective for the alcohol metabolization. This allele is known as ALDH*2, and individuals who have one or two copies of rs671 (A) show more hypersensitivity to alcohol and thus, less dependence on it.

Therefore, homozygous (AA) individuals may have an extreme hypersensitivity reaction to alcohol, which includes facial flushing and severe hangovers, and in turn, have a low probability of developing alcohol dependence. Individuals who present the same allele in heterozygosis (AG), have a functional copy of ALDH2, and therefore may experience a moderate hypersensitivity reaction to alcohol, and in turn, a low probability of developing dependence.

However, individuals homozygous for rs671 (GG genotype) have two functional copies of ALDH2, which implies no hypersensitivity reaction to alcohol.

In a relevant scientific study, the associations between hangover, Asian flush reaction and the increase in mean corpuscular volume (MCV) with the genotype of the enzyme ALDH2 in 251 Japanese workers (139 men, 112 women) were examined.

In this study it was observed that those individuals who presented inactive ALDH2 drank less alcohol than those who presented active ALDH2, but the frequency of the hangover between the two groups for both sexes was not significant. The amount of alcohol consumption that was necessary to produce the hangover was significantly less for men than women. The proportion of men who had a hangover three times or more during the previous year increased significantly with the increase in daily alcohol consumption in men with inactive ALDH2, but not in those with active ALDH2.

202.name: Pigmented rings on the iris
202.description: The iris is the colored part of the eye that surrounds the pupil. As with fingerprints, iris color patterns are very complex and unique to each individual.
202.overview:
The iris is the colored part which surrounds the pupil of the eye. Similar to those of finger prints, the patterns of the iris are very complex and uniquely identify each person. Examples of this the characteristics of the iris include the crypts which are the oval-shaped areas inside of the iris; pigmented rings which surround the iris; furrow contractions, which are radial and concentric bands around the pupil; and pigmented spots on the iris, also called nevi. The heritability of various types of iris patterns ranges between 70-95%, meaning that genetics contribute strongly to these traits.

A person´s iris (colored part of the eye) can say a lot about him/her. Like a fingerprints, iris patterns are highly complex and unique. For this reason, iris recognition is used in some countries for the national identification systems and to automate border crossings.

The human eye

The lens is supported by very fine conjunctive fibers which in turn are joined to the constrictor muscle of the ciliary body. The lens is formed during the third or fourth week of gestation. In children, the lens is bland and elastic but hardens and increases in size as we get older. In a 70 year old person, it is almost three times larger than in a baby.

The vitreous humor, found behind the lens, is a whitish-clear, gelatinous substance filling the central cavity of the eye. This mass is surrounded by the retina, the nerve layer lining the back of the eye. The retina senses light and creates electrical impulses that are sent through the optic nerve to the brain.

The zone that surrounds the optic nerve is the optic disc, an area that does not contain sensory nerve cells and makes up what is known as the blind spot. On the surface of the retina is a small yellowish area lying slightly lateral to the center of the retina that constitutes the region of maximum visual acuity —called also yellow spot.

The iris is the colored part of the eye and can be blue, green, brown, etc., defining the eye color. The pupil, black, is in the center of the iris and the white area around the iris is the sclera. The iris is located behind the cornea, between the anterior chamber and the lens, which it covers in greater or lesser manner depending on its dilation. This anatomical part of the anterior chamber of the eye is in constant motion, permitting the pupil to dilate (mydriasis) or contract (myosis) depending on the intensity of light it receives and regulating the amount of light which reaches the retina.

Each person has a different eye color, with particular nuances and unique shadings. Eye color is a genetic trait determined by the distribution of melanin in the iris by three elements: the melanin of the iris epithelium, the melanin of the anterior part of the iris and the density of the iris stroma. What is your eye color?

The function of the iris

The iris regulates the light that comes into the eye much like the diaphragm of a camera. The iris is comprised of muscular tissue and pigmented cells. If the sphincter muscle of the iris contract, the pupil becomes smaller and allows less light to enter the eye. If these muscles relax or expand, more light comes into the retina. The pigmented cells determine the eye´s color. The more pigment in the iris, the darker the color of the eyes will be. The color of the iris depends on the stromas transparency and the amount of pigmentation that it has. When there is less pigmentation, the eye color will be lighter, blue; when there is more pigmentation, the eye color will be darker and greenish or have brown nuances. This pigmentation is formed during the first months of a baby´s life which explains why all newborn babies have grayish blue eyes and the definite eye color does not occur until the second or third month.

Black eyes

Black colors of the iris do not exist as such as there is always a slight difference between the iris and the pupil. However, Aniridia is an eye disorder that makes the iris seem to disappear, that there is only pupil, giving the appearance of a completely black eye. People with this disorder have reduced vision and increased sensitivity to light (photophobia). They can also develop eye problems such as glaucoma, cataracts, macular degeneration, etc.

Brown eyes

Approximately 60% of the people in the world have brown eyes (clearer or darker, they are all grouped in the same category). This color is due to the large amounts of melanin in the anterior part of the iris. The majority of Asians and Africans have this eye color and according to some studies, because it is a dominant gene, it will extend even more among the world population.

Brownish gold eyes

These can vary between yellowish to golden reddish or coppery color, or even greenish. Lipochrome or yellowish pigment is what makes this color exist. It is also found in green eyes. Although it is not very common, there is no genetic explanation for this color.

Amber eyes

Amber eyes are of a yellowish gold color and result from a predominance of lipochrome in the iris. Like brownish gold eyes, there is no genetic explanation for this color. The difference between the two is that while an amber color is uniform in all of the iris, the brownish gold tone shows small clear color rings around the pupil. Although it is not very common (only 2% of the people in the world have this color eyes), it can be found in European countries.

Hazel eyes

Hazel color eyes falls between brown and green and may contain specks of amber, gold or green. They are more common than amber and usually tend to comprise many other colors, including green, brown and orange. Also, hazel eyes may appear to shift in color and consist of flecks and ripples, making it a very original and variable color.

Green eyes

Green eyes, despite their fame, are found only in about 4% of the population. This color is due to the moderate amounts of melanin in the iris and is an intermediate color between brown and blue. Even though eye color is determined by genetics, it is possible for a person to have light-colored eyes (blue or green) even if the parents have brown eyes. It is infrequent but not a rare color.

Although it is well known that there are no two iris the same, very little else is known of the underlying genetics for these differences. Researchers are studying this subject to learn more about the disorders related to the iris and to provide insights about the brain since the development of the iris and the brain seem to be connected. It is known that on the genetic level, for example, that a mutation tied to a loss in the iris is also associated with defects in the frontal lobe of the brain. Additionally, some characteristics of the iris are correlated to neurologic illnesses such a Down Syndrome.
202.technical_description:
The iris is the coloured part of the eye surrounding the pupil. There is a similarity between individuals’ fingerprints differentiation and iris patterns of each person.

Iris features include crypts, which are oval shaped areas within the iris; Pigmented rings surrounding the pupil; radial and concentric bands or grooves around the pupil; and pigmented spots, also called nevus.

The heritability of various types of iris patterns ranges from 70-95%, which means that the genetic contribute strongly to these features.

In a recently published genome-wide association (GWA) study sought to elucidate the genetic factors involved in the iris variation among people. The researchers measured the pigmentation iris degree and its characteristics in 2,693 individuals, all of European origin.

They found that individuals with genotype rs12896399 (GG) near the SLC24A4 gene had more pronounced pigmentation rings on average compared to individuals with genotype GT, and those with the TT genotype had less pronunciation or even absent. This polymorphism has also been associated with blue eye colour.

The authors found an SNP associated with the presence of crypts, these are areas with oval openings in the iris. This SNP is located near the SEMA3A gene, which encodes a protein that acts both in the iris and the brain and is involved in cell movement. Interestingly, mutations in the SEMA3A gene have been previously linked to neurological disorders such as schizophrenia and Alzheimer's disease.

Another SNP was associated with more pronounced groove contractions, which are circular and radial bands resulting from the iris folds in exactly the same place every time it adapts to different lighting conditions.

A third SNP near the SLC24A4 gene was associated with visible pigmented rings around the pupil and differentiates different shades of green and brown eyes. Interestingly, previous studies have also linked SLC24A4 to differences in eye colour (blue versus green eyes). Since blue iris pigmented rings can cause the eye appear green or hazel, the authors of this study suggest that the presence or absence of pigmented rings can lead the association between eye colours.

The authors found differences by age and sex, showing that older individuals tend to have more groove contractions and pigmented spots in the iris. Men had more crypts than women, but less pigmented spots, contractions of the grooves and rings pigmentation.

508.name: Gene COMT
508.description: The COMT gene is expressed in the brain where it plays a fundamental role in the regulation of neurotransmitters such as dopamine and is also found in other tissues such as liver, kidneys and blood where it participates in the control of the levels of certain hormones.
